title: [Agent UX] Non-markdown file UX: directory listing inclusion + open-in-new-tab for HTML/PDF/images
description:
# Two related gaps

## 1. Links to .html / .pdf / image files should open in new tab

Today when a wiki page contains a link to \`my-report.html\` or \`diagram.svg\`, clicking it replaces the current wiki tab. For rendered-HTML artifacts (mockups, embedded visualizations, standalone reports) the user almost always wants them in a new tab so the wiki context is preserved.

### Proposal

- In the renderer, detect link targets with non-markdown extensions (.html, .pdf, .svg, .png, .jpg, .zip, etc.) and automatically add \`target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\`.
- Alternative / companion: surface a small \"↗ open in new tab\" affordance next to non-markdown file entries in directory listings.
- Out-of-scope for this ticket: fixing Content-Disposition:attachment for .html files served from wikihub origin — that's wikihub-8vk (owner-opt-in rendering).

## 2. Directory listing should include arbitrary non-markdown files as first-class entries

Current folder view (\`app/routes/wiki.py:_folder_listing\` around line 296, rendered by \`folder.html\`) needs to be re-examined — it's unclear whether non-markdown files appear at all, and even if they do, whether they get sensible icons/affordances.

Jacob's use case: published \`docs/mockups/\` with 3 HTML files and 1 markdown index. The HTML files don't show up in a convenient directory browse on the wiki.

### Proposal

- Folder view lists ALL files, not just .md, grouped or sorted by type
- Per-extension icon (📄 for docs, 🖼️ for images, 🌐 for HTML, 📦 for zip, etc.)
- Non-markdown files get: file size, mtime, quick-action (\"↗ open in new tab\", \"⬇ download raw\", depending on Content-Type policy — wikihub-8vk decides that)
- Wikilinks that resolve to non-markdown files render correctly (right now unclear if they do)

### Design question

How does this interact with the \"wiki feels like a markdown encyclopedia\" aesthetic? Options:
- A) Non-md files always visible, mixed chronologically with .md pages
- B) Non-md files tucked under a \"Files\" section at the bottom of each folder view
- C) Per-wiki setting: \"show non-markdown files: inline / separate / hidden\"

Leaning (B) as default with (C) as override — keeps the default view reading like a wiki, not a file browser, but makes assets discoverable.

## Done when

- [ ] Renderer auto-adds target=_blank to links whose targets have non-markdown extensions
- [ ] \`folder.html\` template shows non-md files as first-class entries with appropriate icons + actions
- [ ] \`/agents\` page documents both behaviors
- [ ] Tested with \`jacobcole/picortex/docs/mockups/\` — verify HTML mockup links open in new tab and show up in the directory listing

## Related

- wikihub-8vk (serve-html-with-correct-content-type; owner opt-in rendering)
- Touches: app/renderer.py, app/routes/wiki.py (_folder_listing), templates/folder.html

title: Volume backup strategy for git repos (AWS Lightsail)
description: Persistent volume at /data/repos holds all bare git repos. If Railway recycles or loses the volume, all wiki content is gone (DB has metadata but git content is lost). Need automated backup — e.g. periodic tar to S3/R2, or git bundle push to object storage.

title: Redesign clone URL UI to match GitHub's pattern
description:
## Problem

The current clone UI is a plain "Copy clone URL" button in a dropdown. It copies a bare URL like `https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`. This is unfamiliar to users who expect GitHub's clone dialog pattern, and it doesn't communicate what you're actually getting.

## Design

Replace with a GitHub-style clone popover:

1. **Tabbed protocol selector**: HTTPS (default) | SSH (future) | CLI
2. **Pre-filled command** in a monospace input box:
   - HTTPS tab: `git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`
   - CLI tab: `curl -L https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.zip -o rsi-wiki.zip`
3. **Copy button** (clipboard icon) next to the input
4. **Info box** below: subtle note explaining what you're cloning

## The Two-Repo Problem

WikiHub has two repos per wiki:
- `<slug>.git` — authoritative (owner-only read/write)
- `<slug>-public.git` — derived mirror (everyone else, read-only)

The git backend already handles this transparently — the same URL routes to different repos based on auth:
- Owner with API key → authoritative repo (can push)
- Anyone else → public mirror (read-only, no private pages)

**This is fine.** The URL is the same either way. But the UI should communicate:
- For non-owners: "This clones the public content. Private pages are excluded."
- For owners: "This clones your full wiki including private pages. Use your API key as password."

## Info Box Copy

Non-owner view:
> "Clones public pages only. Private pages are excluded from this view."

Owner view:
> "Use your API key as the password. This includes all pages."

## No push-back problem

Unlike GitHub forks, there's no expectation of pushing back to someone else's wiki via git. The clone is read-only for non-owners (the git backend rejects pushes from non-owners). If someone wants to contribute, they fork via the API/UI, which creates a separate wiki they own. So there's no confusion about "I cloned it but can't push" — it's the expected behavior and matches GitHub's model for repos you don't have write access to.
close_reason: Implemented GitHub-style clone command UI. Info box for public/private context deferred.

title: style sidebar scrollbars: thin, hover-only, no visual clutter
description: the right panel inner scrollbar (and potentially left sidebar scrollbar) look ugly when they appear. fix: use thin scrollbar styling (scrollbar-width: thin, webkit-scrollbar 4px), only visible on hover. both sidebars should use position:sticky and only scroll independently when their content overflows the viewport. the main page scroll should be the primary scroll axis. test on admitsphere/Common-App which has a long left sidebar.

title: MCP server: expose wiki content for AI agent queries
description: instead of RAG with embeddings, expose wiki content via MCP server (model context protocol). any Claude Code or AI agent can connect to a wikihub MCP server and read/search wiki pages natively. the LLM does its own reasoning — no need for us to run embeddings. inspired by Farzapedia's /wiki-query (which just reads files) and gbrain's MCP layer.

title: download button: clarify wiki-level vs page-level download
description: when viewing a single page (not wiki root), the download button offers 'Download ZIP' and 'Copy clone URL' but these download the ENTIRE wiki, not just the current page. this is confusing. fix: (1) make the dropdown text explicit — e.g. 'Download wiki as ZIP' and 'Clone entire wiki'. (2) add a 'Download this page (.md)' option that downloads just the current page's markdown file. the page-level download is just a link to the .md content-negotiated URL.

title: Sidebar: show ACL-granted private pages to authorized non-owners
No rate limiting on login or signup endpoints
description: 10 rapid-fire login attempts with wrong passwords all returned 401 with no throttling. 5 rapid signup attempts all succeeded immediately. An attacker could brute-force passwords or spam-create accounts. Note: API write endpoints have rate limiting (wikihub-un7), but auth endpoints do not.
close_reason: added IP-based rate limiting on login: 20 attempts per 5 minutes per IP, matching existing signup rate limiter pattern

title: RSS feed for wiki changes
description: Atom/RSS feed at /@username/wiki.atom showing recent changes. Enables watch-style workflows without polling. Each commit becomes a feed entry with title=commit message, content=diff summary, link=commit URL. Also consider a global /feed.atom for all public changes (explore-level).

title: Editor and new-page experience is broken — needs full QA pass
description:
The entire editing-a-page-manually and creating-a-new-page web experience is borked. This is P0 — the editor is a core user flow.

Needs investigation:
- Can you create a new page from the web UI? Does the form work?
- Can you edit an existing page? Does the editor load? Does save work?

- Are there JS errors in the console?

description: No way to rename a wiki (change its slug). Need: API endpoint to change slug, rename git repo dirs (both auth and public mirror), update all DB references (wiki.slug, URLs). Should also set up a redirect from old slug to new slug so existing links don't break.

title: [Agent UX] API introspection: GET /api/v1/me/capabilities
description:
# Observed

After auth with wh_*, /api/v1/accounts/me returns {user_id, username, display_name, created_at} — correct but incomplete for agents figuring out what they can do.

What agents need (discoverable from the API, not from /agents HTML):
- Which wikis I own / can write to (with slugs)
- Current rate-limit budgets: writes_per_minute remaining, next reset time
- Feature flags: can I use bulk endpoints? idempotency keys? git push?
- Quota ceilings: max_wikis_per_user, max_pages_per_wiki, max_page_size

# Proposal

\`GET /api/v1/me/capabilities\`

\`\`\`json
{
  \"user\": {\"id\": 4, \"username\": \"jacobcole\"},
  \"wikis\": {\"owned\": [\"picortex\", \"...\"]},
  \"rate_limits\": {
    \"writes\": {\"limit\": 10, \"window_seconds\": 60, \"remaining\": 3, \"reset_at\": \"2026-04-23T20:15:00Z\"}
  },
  \"quotas\": {\"max_wikis\": 50, \"max_page_size_bytes\": 5242880},
  \"features\": {
    \"bulk_pages\": false,
    \"idempotency_keys\": false,
    \"git_push\": true,
    \"content_negotiation\": true
  },
  \"api_version\": \"v1\",
  \"documented_at\": \"https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/agents\"
}
\`\`\`

Agents call this on startup to configure themselves correctly — paginate writes if bulk is off, use idempotency-keys if the feature flag is on, etc. Also great for debugging (\"why am I hitting 429s? oh, my budget is 2 remaining\").

# Related

- wikihub-y3u (rate-limit changes) would update this response
- Feature flags should also live in /llms.txt for LLM-readable discovery

# Done when

- [ ] GET /api/v1/me/capabilities documented + live
- [ ] Returns live rate-limit budget (not just the cap — the remaining)
- [ ] Linked from /agents as \"start here\"

File support roadmap: from image embeds to Google Drive mode
description:
Progressive file support beyond markdown-only browsing. The spec already covers storing binaries in git repos and rendering Obsidian ![[image.png]] embeds, but there's no serving route (images 404) and the sidebar/UI is markdown-only.

**Progressive stages (each unlocks the next):**

Stage 0 — Serve binaries from git repo (bug fix, not feature)
- Route to serve raw files from authoritative/public repo via git cat-file
- Content-type detection, cache headers
- Prerequisite for ![[image.png]] embeds to actually render

Stage 1 — Image attachments (core LLMWiki need)
- Upload images alongside markdown pages
- Inline image preview in editor
- Image gallery or attachment panel per page
- LLM wikis need diagrams, charts, screenshots — this is table stakes

Stage 2 — File browser
- Show all files in sidebar/tree, not just .md
- File type icons
- Non-markdown files visible but clearly secondary to wiki pages

Stage 3 — Non-markdown preview
- Inline PDF viewer, CSV table render, image lightbox
- Reference material browsable alongside wiki pages

Stage 4 — Google Drive mode (future, maybe separate product)
- Drag-drop any file type, bulk upload
- Full-text search across non-markdown files
- Folder-first browsing (vs page-first)
- This diverges from LLMWiki core mission — needs careful product thinking

**Key question:** How far past Stage 1 should we go before it stops being an LLMWiki and starts being a document hosting platform? The Karpathy pattern is markdown knowledge bases maintained by LLMs — images support that, but a Google Drive clone doesn't.

**ListHub contrast:** ListHub is deliberately text-only (flat markdown items, no binary support). WikiHub already went further architecturally (git repos can hold anything), but the serving/browsing layer is markdown-only. The gap is between what git stores and what the UI shows.

**Spec reference:** Binary storage is already specced (wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md, Data Architecture section). This epic covers the serving, browsing, and UX layers on top.

title: Wikipedia-style URLs: replace %20 with underscores
description:
## Context

WikiHub URLs currently use %20 for spaces in page names, making them ugly and hard to share:
`/@jacobcole/wiki/Tools%20%26%20Implementations`

## Research: How other platforms handle spaces

| Platform | Space handling | Example |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| **Wikipedia** | Underscores (`_`) | `/wiki/Machine_learning` |
| **Obsidian Publish** | Plus signs (`+`) | `/garden/My+Great+Note` |
| **GitHub wikis** | Hyphens (`-`) | `/wiki/My-Page` |
| **Most web apps** | `%20` (default) | `/page/My%20Page` |

## Obsidian Publish details
- $8-10/mo per site
- Plus signs for spaces, case-sensitive, no auto-slugification
- Supports `permalink` frontmatter override for custom slugs
- Password protection is all-or-nothing (no per-page ACL)
- No API, no git backing, no social features (fork/star)

## Recommendation: Wikipedia-style underscores

**Why underscores win:**
- Most readable: `The_Pattern` vs `The%20Pattern` vs `The+Pattern`
- Reversible: underscore ↔ space is lossless (filenames rarely have literal underscores)
- Battle-tested: Wikipedia has done this for 25 years, billions of URLs
- Obsidian-compatible: file stays `The Pattern.md` on disk, only URL changes
- Accept all forms: `The_Pattern`, `The%20Pattern`, and `The Pattern` should all resolve

**Implementation:**
- URL generation: `replace(" ", "_")` in all `_page_url()` and template helpers
- URL resolution: `replace("_", " ")` when looking up filenames
- Redirect: `%20` URLs → canonical underscore URLs (301)
- Edge case: files with literal underscores in names — rare, but need a policy (escape? accept ambiguity?)

**Bonus: permalink frontmatter support**
- Steal Obsidian Publish's `permalink` frontmatter field
- Let users set custom URL slugs per-page
- Useful for stable URLs that survive renames

## Ask Harrison
- Does underscore feel right, or would he prefer a different convention?
- Should we also lowercase URLs (case-insensitive matching)?
- How to handle the `&` in names like "Tools & Implementations" — underscore only handles spaces
close_reason: Implemented Wikipedia-style URLs: spaces in page names are now displayed as underscores in URLs (e.g., My_Great_Page instead of My%20Great%20Page). Added url_utils.py for path conversion, updated all route handlers to normalize underscore→space on lookup, added 301 redirects from %20 URLs to canonical underscore URLs, and updated all templates to use the page_url filter.

title: move TOC and backlinks to right sidebar instead of inline
description: currently TOC is at the top and backlinks at the bottom of the article, pushing content down and leaving empty space on the right. consider a right sidebar layout: TOC + backlinks (and later, wikilink graph) in a sticky right column, with a draggable border between the content area and right sidebar. this is a layout-sensitive change — the current two-column layout (left sidebar nav + content) would become three-column (left nav + article + right panel). IMPORTANT: this needs extensive testing on the dev server (localhost:5100) before pushing to production. test at multiple viewport widths, test on pages with no TOC (< 3 headings), test with and without backlinks, test mobile collapse behavior. do NOT push to prod without agent-browser verification at each breakpoint.

title: Curator Agent: inline AI sidebar for wiki curation and navigation
description:
## Vision

An inline AI sidebar on every wiki page — inspired by Neal Stephenson's Librarian (Snow Crash) and the Curator (Ready Player One). A conversational agent that knows who you are, what page you're on, and can take actions on wikis you have access to.

### Simplest use case
You're browsing someone's idea list. You say "promote this to the featured ideas list." The agent reads the current page, writes to the featured wiki (which is public-edit), done.

### Key references
- **Snow Crash Librarian** — already in WikiHub philosophy docs
- **Ready Player One Curator** — guides users through a vast archive
- **Noos project** — Jacob's existing coding-agent-backed knowledge graph (uses Claude Code under the hood)

---

## Architecture: Coding Agent with Filesystem Access (Community Standard)

### Research finding (2026-04-10)

The LLM Wiki ecosystem overwhelmingly uses **coding agents with direct filesystem access**, not API-mediated tool use:

| Pattern | Examples | Share |
|---------|----------|-------|
| **Coding agent (filesystem)** | SamurAIGPT (1.5k★), kfchou, Astro-Han, MehmetGoekce | ~80% |
| **MCP server (API-mediated)** | lucasastorian, sage-wiki | ~15% |
| **Desktop app** | nashsu | ~5% |

Karpathy's gist is literally a CLAUDE.md template — the agent opens a directory, reads the schema, and reads/writes markdown files directly. No API, no database, no custom tools.

### Why this matters for WikiHub

WikiHub's storage is already git repos of markdown files. The Curator doesn't need a custom tool-use layer — it can work on the files directly:

1. **Clone wiki repo** to a temp working directory
2. **Run coding agent** (Claude API with bash+file tools, or Claude Code subprocess) pointed at that directory
3. **Agent operates on markdown files natively** — the exact pattern the community uses
4. **Push changes back** via git push or WikiHub API
5. **Post-receive hook** syncs changes to DB (already exists)

This is simpler AND more capable than building custom API tools, because:
- Agent can do anything with the files (not limited to predefined tools)
- Matches how the entire community already works with LLM wikis
- CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md schema file is the standard discovery mechanism
- WikiHub's per-wiki AGENTS.md already exists and serves this purpose

### Execution model: Claude API with code execution tools

Use the Claude API (not a Claude Code subprocess) with these tools:
- `bash` — run shell commands (scoped to the temp working dir)
- `read_file` — read files in the working dir
- `write_file` — write files in the working dir
- `edit_file` — patch files

This gives coding-agent capabilities without process management complexity. The server:
1. Creates a temp dir per session
2. Clones the relevant wiki repo(s) into it
3. Runs Claude API calls with file/bash tools scoped to that dir
4. On agent actions that modify files, commits and pushes back to WikiHub
5. Cleans up temp dir when session ends

### Sandboxing (MVP vs Production)

| Approach | Isolation | Complexity | When |
|----------|-----------|------------|------|
| **Temp dir + chdir** | Process-level | Low | MVP — sufficient for trusted users |
| **Unix user per session** | User-level | Medium | v1.1 — if multi-tenant |
| **Docker per session** | Container-level | High | v2 — if running untrusted code |

For MVP, a temp directory with the agent's bash tool restricted to that path is adequate. The agent can only read/write files it was given access to.

### Multi-wiki operations

The "promote to featured" use case requires access to TWO wikis (source + destination). The temp dir can contain multiple cloned repos:

```
/tmp/curator-session-abc/
  jacobcole/ideas/          ← cloned from /@jacobcole/ideas
  wikihub/wikihub/          ← cloned from /@wikihub/wikihub (public-edit)
```

The agent's CLAUDE.md tells it the layout and what it can do.

---

## Agent Context (injected as system prompt / CLAUDE.md)

```
You are the Curator, WikiHub's AI librarian.

## Current session
- User: @{username}
- Page: /@{owner}/{wiki}/{page}
- Permissions: {read/write/admin}

## Working directory layout
- {owner}/{wiki}/          ← the wiki you're viewing
- wikihub/wikihub/         ← the main WikiHub wiki (public-edit)
- {other cloned wikis as needed}

## How to make changes
Edit files directly. Changes are auto-committed and pushed to WikiHub.
Follow the wiki's schema.md for structure rules.

## Available wikis for this user
{list of wikis the user can read/write}
```

---

## MVP Scope

### Backend
- `POST /api/v1/agent/chat` — SSE endpoint, requires auth
  - Input: `{message, conversation_id?, page_context?}`
  - Output: SSE stream of agent text + tool call results
  - Creates temp dir, clones relevant repos, runs Claude API with file tools
- Session management: temp dir per conversation, cleanup on timeout
- Git integration: auto-commit + push on file writes

### Frontend
- Slide-out panel on right side of page (does not replace left nav sidebar)
- Toggle button in page actions area (or floating button)
- Chat input at bottom, messages scroll up
- Tool calls shown inline (collapsed by default, expandable)
- "Curator is editing..." indicator when writing
- Desktop only for MVP; hidden on mobile

### System prompt
- Injected with user identity, current page, wiki structure
- References the wiki's own AGENTS.md/schema.md for rules
- Tells agent to work directly with files in the temp dir

---

## Chrome Extension (v2)

A personal Curator that works on any website:
- Sidebar Chrome extension (connects to Cortex project)
- On WikiHub pages: uses the built-in Curator API
- On other sites: personal agent mode (save to your wiki, annotate)
- If built-in sidebar AND extension both present:
  - Extension detects WikiHub, defers to built-in (or takes over — user pref)

---

## Immediate Next Steps
1. Make wikihub/wikihub wiki public-edit
2. Add /api/v1/agent/chat SSE endpoint
3. Implement temp-dir clone + file tools for Claude API
4. Build frontend slide-out panel
5. Test: "promote this idea to featured" end-to-end

## Files involved
- NEW: app/routes/agent_chat.py — SSE chat endpoint + session management
- NEW: app/agent_tools.py — bash/read/write/edit tool definitions for Claude API
- NEW: app/templates/_curator_panel.html — sidebar UI
- MODIFY: app/templates/reader.html — add Curator toggle + panel
- MODIFY: app/__init__.py — register blueprint
- MODIFY: requirements.txt — add anthropic SDK

## Dependencies
- Anthropic API key in server .env
- Claude API with tool use (streaming)
- Git installed on server (already is)

title: Admin toggle to enable/disable Curator chat
description:
Add an admin setting to enable/disable the Curator chat feature, controllable both programmatically and via the admin UI.

## Programmatic
- Env var: CURATOR_ENABLED=true/false (default: false)
- Also stored in DB (admin_settings table or similar) so it can be toggled without restart
- DB setting overrides env var if set

## Admin UI
- Add toggle on the settings/admin page
- Simple on/off switch with label "Curator Agent"
- When off: chat panel doesn't render, /api/v1/agent/chat returns 503
- When on: panel renders on reader pages for authenticated users

## Implementation
- Add CURATOR_ENABLED to app config (read from env + DB)
- Add admin API endpoint: POST /api/v1/admin/settings {curator_enabled: bool}
- Add toggle to admin panel UI (settings.html or admin page)
- Guard the chat endpoint: return 503 if disabled
- Guard the frontend: only render panel HTML if enabled
- Guard by auth: only show to logged-in users (not anonymous visitors)

title: [Agent UX] Bulk page upload endpoint OR git-push path
description:
# Observed

Importing 31 picortex planning docs to jacobcole/picortex required 31 sequential POSTs paced at 7s each (≈4 min wall time) because of the 10/min rate limit + no bulk path. Git-push would have taken 2 seconds.

# Options

## Option A: \`POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/pages/bulk\`

```json
{
  "pages": [
    {"path": "docs/a.md", "content": "...", "visibility": "public"},
    {"path": "docs/b.md", "content": "...", "visibility": "public"}
  ],
  "on_conflict": "skip|overwrite|error"
}
→ 207 Multi-Status
{
  "results": [
    {"path": "docs/a.md", "status": 201, "id": 1234},
    {"path": "docs/b.md", "status": 409, "error": "conflict"}
  ]
}
```

Same ACL checks + git commit batching (one commit per bulk call, not per page). Rate-limit counts the call as one operation, not N.

## Option B: Enable git push to the wiki's bare repo

Wikihub already hosts a bare repo per wiki at \`/@user/wiki.git\` (per README: \"Every wiki is a real git repo — clone, push, blame, bisect\"). If push is authenticated via HTTP basic with API key, agents can:

\`\`\`
git clone https://wh_KEY:@wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/picortex.git
cd picortex
# bulk-copy markdown files
git add -A && git commit -m \"...\" && git push
\`\`\`

This is the most natural bulk path and already aligns with wikihub's \"it's a git repo\" ethos. **Check whether push works today** — README implies it but I didn't test.

## Recommendation

Both. Git push is the idiomatic path; \`/pages/bulk\` is the convenience path for agents that don't want to touch git. Git push probably already works — if it does, this ticket collapses to: (a) confirm it works, (b) document it clearly on /agents, (c) still build \`/pages/bulk\` as a JSON convenience.

# Done when

- [ ] /agents page documents git-push workflow with a working example
- [ ] (if git push doesn't already work) wire up smart HTTP + API-key auth
- [ ] OR (if opting for bulk JSON endpoint) implement /pages/bulk with one-commit-per-call semantics

title: Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[/@user/wiki/page]] syntax
description: Platform-layer cross-wiki linking. Any wikilink starting with /@ resolves across wikis. No git submodules. Resolver checks target page existence across wiki_ids. Unresolved cross-wiki links render red-dashed. Documented in spec 2026-04-10.

title: Auto-convert uploads to Karpathy LLM Wiki format
description:
## Context

When someone uploads a zip of markdown files (e.g., an Obsidian vault, a folder of dated notes, Jeremy Nixon's thinking index), can we auto-detect structure and post-process it into Karpathy's LLM Wiki format?

## Karpathy format target
- `raw/` — immutable source docs
- `wiki/` — LLM-generated/maintained pages with frontmatter (title, tags, status, confidence, contradictions, open_questions)
- `index.md` — content catalog with one-line summaries per page
- `log.md` — append-only activity log
- `schema.md` / `CLAUDE.md` — conventions and domain rules
- Cross-references: symmetric wikilinks, key claims, connections sections

## What we'd need to detect
- Date patterns in filenames or content (daily notes → log.md entries)
- Existing wikilinks `[[...]]` (Obsidian native)
- Folder structure heuristics (e.g., `daily/`, `templates/`, `attachments/`)
- Frontmatter already present vs needs generating
- Which files are "source material" vs "synthesis/wiki pages"

## Open questions
- **Option vs default?** Should this be opt-in ("Convert to LLM Wiki format") or automatic on upload?
- **Destructive or additive?** Do we reorganize their files into raw/wiki/, or just add the missing pieces (index.md, log.md, frontmatter) on top of existing structure?
- **LLM-powered or rule-based?** Simple heuristics might handle 80% (date parsing, wikilink extraction, folder detection). Full Karpathy format (key claims, contradictions, confidence scores) probably needs an LLM pass.
- **When does it run?** On zip upload? On git push? As an async "maintain" action?
- **How does this relate to WikiHub's built-in maintenance vision?** (see llm-wiki-ecosystem wiki — WikiHub Opportunity page)

## Possible approach
1. Rule-based first pass: detect dates, extract wikilinks, generate index.md, infer frontmatter from filenames
2. Optional LLM pass: classify pages as entity/concept/source, generate key claims, find contradictions
3. Surface as "Organize" button on wiki page, or auto-suggest after zip upload

title: Open redirect on login via unvalidated 'next' parameter
description: The login form's 'next' query parameter is followed without validation after successful login. An attacker can craft https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/auth/login?next=https://evil.com and after the user logs in, they're redirected to the attacker's site. Also accepts javascript: URLs. Fix: validate that next is a relative path on the same origin.
close_reason: validated next parameter with urlparse — rejects absolute URLs, protocol-relative URLs, javascript: URIs

title: Clickable page links on history and diff pages
close_reason: File names on history list and commit diff pages are now clickable amber links to the actual pages

title: Pages with only frontmatter title and no markdown heading show no visible title
description: When a page has a title field in YAML frontmatter but no # heading in the markdown body, the rendered page shows no visible heading at all. The browser tab title is correct but the page content has no h1. Verified on @jemoka/knowledge-base/raw/concept/kbhbayes_theorem which has frontmatter title Bayes Theorem but no heading in the rendered output. The frontmatter title should be rendered as an h1 when no explicit heading exists in the content.
close_reason: Closed

title: Signup form not detected by password managers (1Password)
description:
## Problem

The signup / create account page is not recognized by 1Password (and likely other password managers) as an account creation form. 1Password does not offer to autofill or save credentials.

## Likely cause

Password managers detect signup forms via:
- `autocomplete="new-password"` on the password field
- `autocomplete="username"` or `autocomplete="email"` on the identity field
- `<form>` element wrapping the inputs
- Input `name` and `type` attributes (`type="email"`, `type="password"`, `name="email"`)

If any of these are missing or non-standard (e.g., using `<div>` instead of `<form>`, custom input components without proper attributes, or missing `autocomplete` hints), password managers won't detect it.

## Fix

Ensure the signup form has:
1. A `<form>` element wrapping all inputs
2. `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` for the email field
3. `<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="new-password">` for the password field
4. `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` for the username/display name field
5. A `<button type="submit">` (not just an onclick handler)

Test with 1Password browser extension after fixing — it should offer to save new credentials on submit.

title: Portable-self structure: distinguish always-loaded context from on-demand accessible content (Claude.md vs Claude skills, applied to WikiHub)
description:
The bootstrap-by-URL+key pattern (wikihub-4rel) gives an agent everything reachable. But not all of "everything" should be loaded into context every turn — that's wasteful and crowds the window. We need an explicit split, mirroring the Claude.md vs Claude-skills distinction, applied to WikiHub-as-context.

Research is happening in parallel: see ~/memory/research/agent-context-structures-2026-04-23.md (Claude memory model, OpenClaw, RAG patterns). Update the design here once that lands.

## The split (proposed)

**Tier 1 — Always loaded (the "soul" / always-in-context layer)**
- A small curated dump that goes into the agent's system prompt every conversation
- Token budget: ~500–2000 tokens
- Contents: who Jacob is, current focus, top-level preferences, vocabulary/identity, the index of what's available in tier 2/3
- Source: a designated wiki page, e.g. `@jacob/portable-self/soul.md` (name TBD pending research synthesis)

**Tier 2 — Skills / declarative tool definitions**
- YAML-frontmattered markdown files describing capabilities the agent can invoke
- Loaded by metadata only (name + when-to-use); body loaded only when the skill is invoked
- Source: a designated wiki folder, e.g. `@jacob/portable-self/skills/`
- Mirrors Claude Code's `~/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md` pattern

**Tier 3 — RAG / on-demand knowledge**
- Full pages the agent retrieves only when relevant to the current turn
- Source: any wiki the scoped key has access to (per wikihub-gzlt)
- Retrieval via existing /api/v1/search + /api/v1/wikis/.../pages

## Concrete WikiHub artifacts (proposed)

A new convention: the user has a wiki named `@me/portable-self` with structure:
```
soul.md              # Tier 1 — always loaded
skills/
  schedule.md        # Tier 2 — load body on invocation
  contacts.md
  ...
notes/               # Tier 3 — RAG retrievable
journal/             # Tier 3
research/            # Tier 3
```

The `bootstrap` endpoint (wikihub-4rel) is updated to recognize this convention:
- If a wiki named `portable-self` exists for the user, its `soul.md` is included verbatim in the bootstrap response under a top-level `always_loaded` key
- The `skills/` directory listing (file names + frontmatter only, NOT bodies) is included under `skills`
- Other content is reachable via the regular API but not pre-loaded

## Why a wiki convention (not a separate config file)

- Jacob already curates wikis. No new tool to learn.
- Per-page visibility means the same `portable-self` wiki can be public OR scoped — agents see only what their key permits.
- Editing the wiki updates the agent's context — single source of truth.
- Public alternative: any user can publish their own portable-self for others to import.

## Key design question (for research synthesis)

Should `soul.md` be:
(a) A free-form prose page Jacob writes himself (highest fidelity, no constraints)
(b) Frontmatter-driven (`identity:`, `preferences:`, `focus:`, `vocabulary:` keys — easier for agents to parse, harder for Jacob to write fluidly)
(c) Hybrid (frontmatter for structured fields, prose body for everything else)

Recommend (c), but defer to research findings.

## Wiki page convention name

Open Q: `portable-self`? `agent-context`? `me`? `bio`? Whichever wins, register it in agent-discovery surfaces (wikihub-4rel) so any agent connecting via bootstrap knows where to look.

## Acceptance

- This ticket WRITES THE PLAN; implementation tickets fan out from it once research lands
- Naming convention chosen and documented in `docs/agents/portable-self.md`
- Bootstrap endpoint enhanced to recognize the convention
- A reference `@jacob/portable-self` wiki populated as the canonical example
- /agents page documents the three-tier split for users curating their own portable-self

## Dependencies
- BLOCKED-INFORMS-BY: wikihub-XXXXX (research on agent context structures, in flight)
- RELATED: wikihub-4rel (bootstrap endpoint will surface this), wikihub-gzlt (scoped keys gate the access tiers)
- RELATED: wikihub-fuj (contribute-back from AI conversations) — async write path back into the same wiki

## Out of scope
- Writing the actual content of Jacob's `portable-self` wiki — that's a separate curation task once the convention exists
- Cross-user portable-self imports ("clone Karpathy's portable self") — interesting future, not v1
notes:
## Research synthesis (2026-04-23) — concrete decisions

Full report: `~/memory/research/agent-context-structures-2026-04-23.md` (216 lines, 11 sources, ID-RAG paper found).

**Adopt OpenClaw's filename convention verbatim.** OpenClaw is the most explicit standard (162 community templates drop-in compatible at `github.com/mergisi/awesome-openclaw-agents`), and its split is a superset of Claude Code's. ID-RAG (MIT paper, arxiv 2509.25299, Sept 2025) is the academic framing of exactly this split — endorses structured queryable identity store, retrieval per decision loop, identity separate from episodic memory.

### Final naming

A user's `@user/portable-self` wiki has this layout:

```
SOUL.md              # Tier 1 — agent identity (~300-500 tok)
                     #   name, values, communication style, behavioral guardrails.
                     #   Stable across users.
USER.md              # Tier 1 — who the human is (~500-1500 tok)
                     #   name, role, current focus, preferences, relationships, projects.
                     #   This is the CLAUDE.md-equivalent personal block.
AGENTS.md            # Tier 1 — operating procedures (~500-1500 tok)
                     #   workflows, conventions, "always do X" rules.
TOOLS.md             # Tier 1 — tool reach-for guide (~200-500 tok, optional)
MEMORY.md            # Tier 1 — auto-written learnings index (≤200 lines cap)

memory/
  YYYY-MM-DD.md      # Tier 3 — daily logs; today + yesterday auto-read,
                     #   older retrieved via memory_search
  topics/
    <topic>.md       # Tier 3 — auto-written topic detail files
skills/
  <skill-name>/
    SKILL.md         # Tier 2 — Anthropic's YAML-frontmatter skill spec
                     #   (lowercase-hyphen name, description ≤1024 chars,
                     #    body <5k tok). Metadata always loaded; body on trigger.
    resources/...    # Tier 3 — bundled scripts/refs, read on demand
wiki/                # Tier 3 — RAG-retrievable pages (the bulk of WikiHub content)
```

Aggregate Tier 1 budget: **~3-8K tokens.** Per-file cap: 20K characters (mirrors OpenClaw).

### Decisions resolved (no longer open questions)

- **Convention name:** `portable-self` ✓ (replaces earlier candidates `agent-context`, `me`, `bio`)
- **`soul.md` format:** hybrid — frontmatter for structured identity fields when useful, prose body for everything else. Matches both OpenClaw and Letta core-memory persona block.
- **Use `AGENTS.md` not `CLAUDE.md`** as the procedure filename — Anthropic itself recommends `AGENTS.md` for cross-tool compatibility; `CLAUDE.md` signals tool-lock-in.
- **Keep `USER.md` separate from `SOUL.md`.** Both Letta (persona vs human blocks) and OpenClaw split these. Agent identity stays stable when the user changes; user facts can be edited without rewriting the soul.

### Bootstrap endpoint contract (refines wikihub-4rel)

`GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` returns:

```json
{
  "user": { ... },
  "scope": { ... },
  "always_loaded": {
    "soul":    "<full SOUL.md content>",
    "user":    "<full USER.md content>",
    "agents":  "<full AGENTS.md content>",
    "tools":   "<full TOOLS.md content or null>",
    "memory":  "<MEMORY.md head — first 200 lines>"
  },
  "skills": [
    { "name": "...", "description": "...", "path": "skills/<name>/SKILL.md" }
  ],
  "wikis":     [ ... per-wiki summaries ... ],
  "discovery": { ... API entrypoints ... }
}
```

The agent stuffs `always_loaded.*` into its system prompt verbatim. Skills go in by metadata only; bodies are fetched via standard page reads when triggered. Wikis are RAG.

### ID-RAG note (forward-looking, NOT v1)

ID-RAG suggests a structured identity *graph* queried per decision loop, not per session. For long-horizon persona coherence (multi-day, multi-context conversations), this matters. We can add an `identity/<entity>.md` directory later for this without restructuring v1. Defer until drift is observed.

### What NOT to do (from the report's "skip" list)

- Don't invent a new word for SOUL (e.g., `core.md`, `persona.md`). `SOUL.md` is the dominant convention.
- Don't merge `USER.md` into `SOUL.md`.
- Don't put `wiki/` in bootstrap. RAG only.
- Don't use `CLAUDE.md` as a filename.

### Contrarian note worth weighing

Claude Code deliberately collapses everything into `CLAUDE.md`. That works for task-scoped coding agents. For a persistent personal agent against a knowledge base, OpenClaw's explicit split is better — identity stable, user facts churn, procedures evolve independently. We're in OpenClaw's regime.

## Caveats

- OpenClaw primary docs are thin; the 8-file bootstrap list reconciles across 4+ secondary sources and 2 GitHub issues but lacks a single authoritative first-party page listing all eight.
- ID-RAG is one paper, single benchmark (election sim). Treat empirical claims as directional; the conceptual contribution (identity as queryable graph distinct from episodic memory) is what's worth borrowing.

title: Serve binary files from git repo (images 404)
description:
![[image.png]] Obsidian embeds are supported in the renderer but there's no route to actually serve binary files from the git repo. Images referenced in markdown pages 404.

Needs a route that serves raw files from the authoritative or public mirror repo via git cat-file, with proper content-type detection and cache headers. Should respect the same auth dispatch as other wiki reads (owner gets authoritative repo, everyone else gets public mirror).

This is Stage 0 of the file support roadmap (wikihub-291) — prerequisite for any image/attachment functionality to work.

ACL-aware: respects per-file visibility.

title: Bootstrap-by-URL+key: any agent gets full WikiHub context from a single connection string
description:
Today an agent that wants to use WikiHub has to: read /agents docs, learn auth, hit /api/v1/accounts, store a key, then re-implement common ops (read page, write page, list wikis). It works but it's not "drop-in" — there's friction every time someone wires WikiHub into a new agent framework.

Goal: any portable agent (a generic Claude/ChatGPT/custom agent runtime) should be able to add WikiHub support in ~5 lines AND mint a scoped key for less-trusted contexts (group chat, embedded contexts, shared-with-friends agent). This is the WikiHub-side counterpart to the user's portable-agent project.

## Scope (this ticket)

1. **Scoped-key minting endpoint** is provided by the parent feature: wikihub-XXX (scoped/restricted API keys — see sibling ticket). This ticket DEPENDS on that.

2. **`/api` discovery doc** (just shipped, wikihub-75e7) gains a `keys.scoped` link pointing at the scoped-key endpoint, so an agent that hits the discovery root learns about scoping in one round-trip.

3. **Reference Python client** — a tiny package (or single module under `cli/wikihub_client/`) with the bare minimum:
   ```python
   from wikihub_client import WikiHub
   wh = WikiHub(api_key="wh_...")
   wh.read("@me/notes/index.md")
   wh.write("@me/notes/new.md", "# Hello")
   wh.list_wikis()
   wh.mint_scoped_key(label="group-chat", max_visibility="public-edit", read_only=True, expires_in="7d")
   wh.feedback(kind="bug", subject="...", body="...")
   ```
   Distribute via PyPI as `wikihub-client` (separate from `wikihub-cli`, which is the user-facing CLI). Keep it under ~200 lines — agents pulling it in shouldn't pay for a heavy dep.

4. **Reference Node/TS client** — same surface, same name (`@wikihub/client`). Mirrors the Python package. The MCP server is already TypeScript so this can share types with `mcp-server/`.

5. **`docs/agents/portable-agent.md`** — a ~1-page recipe walking through:
   - "I have an agent. How do I add WikiHub in 5 lines?"
   - "How do I run my agent in DM mode (full key) vs group mode (scoped key)?"
   - The actual code snippets in Python + TS
   - When to mint a fresh scoped key per session vs. reuse one

6. **Update agent-discovery surfaces** (per AGENTS.md sync table: /AGENTS.md route, /llms.txt, /agents HTML, /.well-known/wikihub.json) to advertise the client packages and the portable-agent recipe.

## Why P2

Real value, but blocked by the scoped-keys feature. After that lands this becomes a 1-2 day docs+packaging effort.

## Open Q (for Jacob)
- **Should the reference client be vendored into the agent's repo, or pulled from PyPI/npm?** PyPI/npm is more standard but adds an install step. Vendoring is zero-friction but creates drift. Recommend: PyPI/npm with a copy-paste fallback in the docs.
- **What's the user's portable agent built on?** (Claude Agent SDK, custom, OpenAI Assistants?) — if there's a specific framework, we should ship a framework-specific binding alongside the generic client.

## Dependencies
- BLOCKED by the scoped-API-keys ticket
- Aligns with: wikihub-1ry (capabilities), wikihub-75e7 (/api discovery), wikihub-o6d (OpenAPI), the existing mcp-server
- Should NOT duplicate the MCP server's surface — MCP is for tool-call agents; this client is for agents that orchestrate WikiHub calls directly without an MCP layer

## Acceptance
- A new agent can `pip install wikihub-client` and write a working integration in <10 lines
- Calling `wh.mint_scoped_key(...)` returns a usable scoped `wh_*` key
- `docs/agents/portable-agent.md` exists and is linked from /agents and /llms.txt
- /api discovery doc advertises the scoped-key endpoint and the client packages
notes:
## Decisions (Jacob, 2026-04-23)

**Reframe.** This is NOT a Python/TS SDK ticket primarily. It's a **bootstrap pattern** ticket. The goal:

> Give any agent (OpenClaw, Claude Code, custom, anything) a single URL+key and it should immediately be able to fetch all the user's context.

Framework-independent. No Claude-specific bindings, no OpenAI-specific bindings. The agent runtime just needs HTTP.

## Canonical share-string format

A single copy-pasteable connection string the user hands to any agent:

```
https://wikihub.md/?key=wh_<scoped-key>
```

OR (preferred — basic-auth style, harder to leak via referrer header / browser history):

```
https://wh_<scoped-key>@wikihub.md/
```

Pick one canonical form (basic-auth-style preferred) and document it. Any agent reading the string can:
- Parse host (`wikihub.md`) and key
- Hit `GET /api/v1/me/capabilities` → knows scope, wikis available, rate limits
- Hit `GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` (new endpoint, see below) → gets full snapshot in one round-trip

## NEW: GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap

Single-shot endpoint that returns everything an agent needs to "know who I'm working for" in one fetch:

```json
{
  "user": { "id": ..., "username": "jacob", "display_name": "..." },
  "scope": { ... echo of capabilities scope, or null for full keys ... },
  "wikis": [
    {
      "slug": "@jacob/notes",
      "title": "...",
      "description": "...",
      "visibility": "...",
      "page_count": 47,
      "recent_pages": [{"path": "...", "title": "...", "updated_at": "..."}, ... up to 10],
      "index_page": {"path": "index.md", "excerpt": "first 500 chars"} | null
    },
    ...
  ],
  "rate_limits": { ... },
  "discovery": {
    "api_root": "/api",
    "openapi": "/api/v1/openapi.json",
    "feedback": "/api/v1/feedback"
  }
}
```

So the agent's onboarding becomes literally: read the connection string → one HTTP call → it has everything.

## Settings UI: "Connect an agent" button

In /settings, alongside the scoped-keys section, add a **Connect an agent** button. It opens a modal:
- Form: label, scope chips (visibility ceiling, wiki allowlist), expires-in dropdown, read-only toggle
- Submit → mints a scoped key (via `POST /api/v1/keys/scoped` from gzlt)
- Modal then shows the connection string in a big monospace box with a Copy button
- Subtitle: "Paste this into your agent. The key is shown once — store it now."
- Also a small QR code for mobile-paste workflows

This is the human ergonomics part — most users won't curl `POST /api/v1/keys/scoped` directly.

## Out of scope (for this ticket)

- Reference Python/Node client libraries — separate, optional, lower priority. Most agent runtimes can do raw HTTP fine. If users ask, file as follow-ups.
- Framework-specific bindings (Claude Agent SDK, OpenAI Assistants, etc.) — explicitly NOT shipping these. The HTTP API + bootstrap endpoint is the universal interface.
- MCP integration — the MCP server already exists for agents that prefer that protocol. This ticket is for agents that just want HTTP.

## Acceptance

- New `GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` endpoint returns the snapshot JSON above
- Settings UI has a "Connect an agent" button that mints a scoped key and produces a copy-pasteable connection string
- `docs/agents/connect.md` documents the connection string format and the one-call bootstrap flow with curl examples
- /api discovery doc and /llms.txt advertise both the bootstrap endpoint and the connection-string format
- An OpenClaw agent (or any other) given the connection string can fetch the bootstrap and start working with no other configuration

title: Rename visibility levels for clarity: public→public-view, unlisted→unlisted-view
description:
## Problem

The current visibility names are confusing. 'public' doesn't make it clear that it means read-only (no editing). Users could reasonably think 'public' means anyone can edit.

## Proposed Naming

| Current | New | Meaning |
|---------|-----|---------|
| public | public-view | Anyone can read, only owner can edit |
| public-edit | public-edit | Anyone can read AND edit |
| unlisted | unlisted-view | Only people with the link can read |
| private | private | Only owner/ACL can read |

## Scope

- Update the `VALID_VISIBILITIES` set in `app/acl.py`
- Update all references in routes, templates, models
- Update frontmatter parsing to accept both old and new names (backwards compat)
- Update the visibility badge labels in reader.html, folder.html
- Update API docs
- Migration: existing pages with 'public' should map to 'public-view', 'unlisted' to 'unlisted-view'
- Keep accepting old names in frontmatter for backwards compatibility (Postel's Law)

## Files involved
- `app/acl.py` — VALID_VISIBILITIES
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py` — visibility endpoints
- `app/templates/reader.html` — visibility badges
- `app/templates/folder.html` — visibility badges
- `app/models.py` — any visibility defaults
- `wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md` — update spec

## Notes
Candidate for Codex — thorough rename across codebase.

title: Review: Editorial Picks on Explore — how should curation work?
description:
Currently Editorial Picks on /explore is hardcoded to show @wikihub/wiki as a single wiki card. This was auto-generated code, not a deliberate product decision.

Options:
1. **Current: hardcoded @wikihub/wiki card** — just shows the official wiki. Not really 'editorial picks.'
2. **Pages from @wikihub/wiki as picks** — the Featured Wikis page we created has curated links. Could render those as cards instead of showing the wiki container.
3. **Tag/flag system** — wikis get an 'editorial_pick' flag, admins toggle it. Explore queries for flagged wikis.
4. **Curated from the community index wiki** — @wikihub/wikihub Featured Wikis page is the source of truth. A special tag or frontmatter field marks wikis for the explore page.
5. **Remove editorial section entirely** — just show popular (by stars) and recent. Let the @wikihub wiki be the editorial venue, not the explore page.

The community index wiki (@wikihub/wikihub/Featured Wikis) already exists as editorial curation. The question is whether /explore should also have its own editorial section or just link to the community wiki.

title: Sidebar: highlight too wide + child alignment off
description:
## RECURRING ISSUE — sidebar indentation

⚠️ CHECK THIS whenever touching sidebar CSS or templates.

### Current problem
Child items under folders don't appear visually nested enough. padding-left: 12px on .sidebar-children is insufficient — needs ~20px.

### Files affected
- app/templates/reader.html
- app/templates/folder.html  
- app/templates/profile.html

- 2026-04-10: Fixed highlight overflow (overflow-x: hidden, flex constraints) but indentation still too shallow

3. Verify highlight background doesn't overflow sidebar width
4. Compare with Notion's sidebar indentation as reference

title: [Agent UX] Public issue-posting HTTP API + GitHub Issues/beads sync w/ approval gate
description:
# Context

Internally wikihub's Curator agent already has a `create_issue` tool (`app/routes/agent_chat.py:696`) that files roadmap issues with `{title, issue_type, priority, description}`. But there is NO external HTTP endpoint for agents/integrations/humans to post an issue directly against a wiki.

Jacob's ask: "if there's an API to literally have it go into the beads. Maybe we could have issues sync into the beads or something, though that probably should be subject to approval so you don't have injection."

# Three layers to design

## Layer 1: Public REST API for issue creation

Propose:
```
POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/issues
Authorization: Bearer wh_...
{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "...",
  "issue_type": "feature|bug|task|epic|chore",
  "priority": 0-4,
  "source": {"kind": "api|github|webhook|human", "ref": "..."}
}
→ 201 {"id": "...", "url": "/@owner/slug/issues/..."}
```

Require the authenticated user to have write access to the wiki (reuse `_get_wiki_or_404` + ACL check). Issues are stored in the existing roadmap-issues table (wherever Curator agent writes today).

## Layer 2: GitHub Issues integration (bidirectional, optional per wiki)

Per wiki settings:
- **Mirror mode (sync-out)**: wikihub issues → GitHub issues on a configured repo (webhook-driven). Safe — wikihub is the authority.
- **Ingest mode (sync-in)**: GitHub issues → wikihub staging queue (see Layer 3). NOT auto-approved because untrusted external input.

Store PAT / GitHub App credentials per wiki, encrypted. Surface in wiki Settings page.

## Layer 3: Beads sync with approval gate (the prompt-injection safety)

Jacob's concern: "subject to approval so you don't have injection."

Design:
- Incoming issues from the public API (or GitHub ingest) land in a `staging_issues` table with `approved=false`
- Owner sees a pending-issues dashboard (`/@owner/slug/issues/pending`)
- On approve, issue copies into wikihub's internal store AND optionally emits to beads via `bd create` (run on the wikihub server against a configured beads repo path)
- Approval has a `review_hint` free-text field so the owner can add "bd-prefix: picortex" or "close-as-dup: wikihub-xxx" before ingest
- Rejection tombstones the row with reason

Alternative: make approval auto for issues posted by the wiki owner's own API key, manual for everyone else.

# Open questions

- Do we want `issues` as a first-class wikihub concept (new table/routes/UI), or keep piggybacking on the Curator agent's internal roadmap store?
- Can we reuse beads' git-backed format directly — e.g. wikihub maintains a thin `.beads/issues.jsonl` file inside each wiki's git repo? That would give git-native sync without extra plumbing. Promising.
- GitHub App vs per-wiki PAT — the App is slicker but much more setup work.

# Done when

- [ ] `POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/issues` documented + live
- [ ] GitHub mirror works one-way (wikihub → GH) for one test wiki
- [ ] Staging queue + approval UI for incoming issues from non-owner sources
- [ ] Optional beads `.beads/issues.jsonl` write on approval

# Related

- `app/routes/agent_chat.py:696-745` (existing create_issue tool schema)
- Jacob's prompt-injection concern: untrusted external issue body could contain instructions aimed at agents reading the issue later → staging + explicit human approval is the answer
- Beads repo format: JSONL per-project in `.beads/issues.jsonl` + `.beads/metadata.json`

title: Landing page: 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' CTA
description:
Consider adding secondary CTAs on the landing page hero alongside 'Publish a wiki in seconds'. Candidates:

1. **'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity'** — the grand vision, taps into Snow Crash / Librarian philosophy. wikihub isn't just a tool, it's a collective knowledge project.

2. **'Make private collaborative lists with agents'** — the practical agent use case. Not everything is public publishing — private wikis where your agent writes and organizes alongside you.

3. **'Agent-native Google Drive'** — positions wikihub as the file system / knowledge layer that agents can actually read and write to, unlike Google Drive. Markdown files in git = structured, versionable, API-accessible. Google Drive is for humans with browsers; wikihub is for humans AND agents. This framing resonates with the 'why not just use Notion/Google Docs' objection — those tools have terrible agent APIs.

These could be secondary CTAs, tagline variants, rotating hero text, or value prop cards below the fold. The key insight: wikihub has three distinct value props for three audiences:
- **Publishers** → 'Publish a wiki in seconds' (Karpathy wave, Obsidian users)
- **Visionaries** → 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' (knowledge builders, researchers)
- **Agent users** → 'Agent-native Google Drive' or 'Collaborate with agents on private knowledge' (developers, AI power users)

title: [Agent UX] Serve non-markdown files with correct Content-Type; owner opt-in to render HTML instead of attachment
description:
# Observed

Deployed wikihub at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai returns .html files with:
  content-type: application/octet-stream
  content-disposition: attachment; filename=\"<name>.html\"

Example: \`GET /@jacobcole/picortex/docs/mockups/00-platonic-ideal.html\` → download prompt in Safari instead of rendering.

Local \`app/routes/wiki.py\` line 803-827 looks correct — it calls \`mimetypes.guess_type(page_path)\` and returns whatever type that gives (text/html for .html). Only PDF gets Content-Disposition set (inline). So deployed code is either:
- Diverged from git (my local clone was last updated 2026-04-12; server may have newer code), OR
- Has middleware / nginx / Cloudflare rule adding Content-Disposition:attachment

# Why it matters

Agents and users publishing rendered docs, mockups, or interactive SVG naturally want to host .html files. Today the workaround is to push to GitHub Pages instead (I did that for picortex mockups → now at https://tmad4000.github.io/picortex/). Wikihub should be able to do this natively.

# But security concerns are real

Serving attacker-controlled HTML from wikihub.globalbr.ai origin = potential XSS against the SSO session cookie of other wikihub users. The \`attachment\` disposition is probably deliberate defense.

# Proposal: owner opt-in with isolated subdomain

Ship the render-HTML feature safely:

1. **Per-wiki setting** \`render_html: true\` (default false). Owner must explicitly enable for their wiki.
2. **Served from a cookie-less subdomain**: e.g. \`raw.wikihub.globalbr.ai/<owner>/<slug>/<path>\` (or \`<hash>.raw.wikihub.globalbr.ai\` for stronger isolation). The main wikihub SSO cookie is not sent to this origin, so malicious HTML can't steal sessions.
3. Main wiki page links to the raw-subdomain URL when render_html is on.
4. Still serves with proper \`Content-Type: text/html\` + a strict \`Content-Security-Policy\` that blocks scripts from loading user cookies / cross-origin requests to the main origin.

Alternative simpler approach: add an \`?render=1\` query param on the main origin that strips Content-Disposition but requires the wiki owner to explicitly opt in via settings. No subdomain needed but slightly weaker isolation.

# Immediate workaround for agents

Document on /agents: \"HTML files are stored but served as download. For rendered artifacts, host on an external origin (GitHub Pages, jacobcole.ai, etc.) and link from a markdown page.\"

# Done when

- [ ] Per-wiki \`render_html\` setting exists in DB + UI toggle
- [ ] Raw subdomain OR safe query param serves .html / .svg / .css / .js as their natural mime types when enabled
- [ ] /agents page documents the feature + the default (downloads-for-safety)
- [ ] E2E test covers a .html file rendering correctly when opted-in

# Related

- app/routes/wiki.py:803-827 (non-md serving logic — local code looks correct, verify deployed diverged)
- wikihub-o6d (Content-Type + openapi.json discoverability)
- wikihub-8dz (Cloudflare WAF blocks — adjacent origin/headers work)

title: WebSocket support for xterm.js terminal and Curator chat
description:
## Problem

The admin terminal (xterm.js) uses HTTP polling (100ms GET + POST per keystroke), causing ~200ms lag per character. The Curator chat uses SSE which ties up a gunicorn sync worker.

## Solution

Add WebSocket support via flask-sock. This gives:
1. Real-time terminal I/O (admin auth page)
2. Real-time Curator chat streaming (instead of SSE)
3. Foundation for future real-time features

## Requirements
- Add flask-sock to requirements.txt
- Switch gunicorn to gevent workers: `gunicorn -k gevent`
- WebSocket endpoint for terminal: /ws/terminal
- WebSocket endpoint for Curator chat: /ws/curator
- Update xterm.js to use WebSocket attach addon
- Update Curator panel JS to use WebSocket instead of fetch+SSE

## Risk
Changing gunicorn worker type from sync to gevent could affect request handling. Test all existing endpoints after the switch.

title: Curator Agent: inline sidebar agent for wiki curation (Snow Crash / Ready Player One inspired)
description:
## Vision

An inline curator agent sidebar in the spirit of the Librarian from Snow Crash and the Curator from Ready Player One. An AI assistant that lives in the WikiHub UI sidebar and can take actions on your behalf — curate, cross-reference, promote content between wikis.

## Example Use Case

You're browsing someone's idea list. You want to promote an idea to the Featured Wikis page. The curator agent can:
1. Read the current page context (who you are, what page you're on)
2. Take actions with your permissions (read/write wikis you have access to)
3. Append content to public-edit pages, create pages in your own wikis, etc.

## Architecture Considerations

### Agent Backend
- **Claude Code under the hood** (like Noos project) — coding agent with file/API tools
- Should run in **print mode** for streaming responses to the sidebar
- Needs the current user's API key for WikiHub API calls
- Must be aware of: logged-in user, current page URL, current wiki context

### Security / Isolation
- Agent runs with the permissions of the current user (their API key)
- Must NOT get access to wrong directories on the server
- **MVP: Unix user permissions** may be sufficient — each agent session runs as a restricted user with only API access (no filesystem access beyond temp dirs)
- **Future: Docker containers** for full isolation if needed
- Agent should only interact via the WikiHub API, not touch git repos directly

### Parallel Agents
- Architecture should support multiple users running curator agents simultaneously
- Each agent session is stateless — gets user context at start, runs, returns result
- Server-side: queue or spawn model per request

### UI
- Sidebar panel on the right side of wiki pages
- Chat-like interface: user types a request, agent streams a response
- Agent can show proposed changes before applying them
- Minimizable/collapsible

### Chrome Extension (v2)
- Personal agent sidebar that works on any website
- If Chrome extension sidebar is open, WikiHub's built-in sidebar hides (avoid duplication)
- Extension would use the same WikiHub API key
- Relates to the Cortex project for personal agent infrastructure
- Could detect if user is logged into WikiHub and auto-configure

## MVP Scope (v1)
1. Right sidebar panel on wiki pages (behind feature flag or logged-in only)
2. Chat input → sends to Claude API with system prompt including page context
3. Agent can call WikiHub API endpoints (search, read_page, create_page, update_page, append_section)
4. Streams response back to sidebar
5. No filesystem access — API-only

## Open Questions
- Unix permissions vs Docker for agent isolation?
- Should the agent have its own API key or use the user's?
- Rate limiting per user?
- How to handle the Chrome extension ↔ built-in sidebar coordination?

title: Add /api root endpoint: agent-friendly discovery index
description:
Today the API is mounted at /api/v1 (app/__init__.py:34). Hitting /api returns 404, which confuses agents that probe the obvious root before discovering versioned paths. They guess wrong endpoints, retry, sometimes give up.

## Fix

Add a GET /api endpoint that returns a small JSON discovery doc:

```json
{
  "name": "wikihub",
  "current_version": "v1",
  "versions": {
    "v1": {
      "base": "/api/v1",
      "openapi": "/api/v1/openapi.json",
      "capabilities": "/api/v1/me/capabilities",
      "docs": "/docs/api"
    }
  },
  "deprecated_versions": [],
  "feedback": "/api/v1/feedback",
  "request_id_header": "X-Request-ID"
}
```

Also serve the same doc at /api/ (trailing slash) and HEAD on both. Content-Type: application/json. Cacheable (5 min) since it changes rarely.

## Why P1

Trivial implementation, large agent-UX dividend. Pairs naturally with wikihub-o6d (OpenAPI) — this gives agents a known fixed entry point to discover everything else.

## Files
- new route in app/routes/api.py (or a dedicated app/routes/api_root.py)
- register at root (no /api/v1 prefix) — likely in app/__init__.py

## Acceptance
- curl https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/api → 200, JSON with the schema above
- curl https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/api/ → same response
- HEAD /api → 200 with same headers, no body
- Linked from README.md API section

title: [Agent UX] Cloudflare WAF blocks non-curl user-agents (error 1010)
description:
# Observed

During the 2026-04-23 picortex session, the wikihub bulk-upload from Python urllib (default UA \`Python-urllib/3.12\`) was blocked with Cloudflare **error 1010** on every /api/v1/wikis/.../pages request — \`HTTP 403\` with a Cloudflare block page as the body (\"The owner of this website has banned your access based on your browser's signature\").

The exact same request payloads sent via **curl** (UA \`curl/8.x\`) succeeded.

# Why this matters

Python is the default HTTP client for many agent frameworks — Anthropic's SDK, LangChain, Instructor, etc. Any agent that isn't Claude Code itself (which happened to use curl) will hit this wall before it even sees a rate-limit, key-scope, or API-shape issue. The Cloudflare block returns a generic HTML page, not a JSON error, so agents get a confusing 403 with no machine-readable reason.

# Likely cause

Cloudflare WAF \"Super Bot Fight Mode\" or \"Bot Fight Mode\" blocks known programmatic user-agents by default. \`Python-urllib\` is in the default bad-bot signature set; \`curl\` is not.

# Options

1. **Allowlist in Cloudflare WAF** — exempt /api/v1/* from bot-fight rules (or entire wikihub.globalbr.ai). Risk: more scraping headroom, but the API is authenticated anyway so the bot-fight protection is redundant there.
2. **Require API key in query/header**: keep WAF strict but add a WAF bypass rule for requests carrying a valid \`Authorization: Bearer wh_*\` header. Needs a Cloudflare Worker or custom rule.
3. **Document the workaround** in /agents: \"If your HTTP client uses a generic Python/Node user-agent, set a custom User-Agent header explicitly (e.g. \`User-Agent: my-agent/1.0 (+wikihub-api)\`).\"

Recommend (1) scoped to /api/v1/*, plus (3) as a doc update until (1) is shipped.

# Done when

- [ ] /api/v1/* works with default Python urllib UA
- [ ] /agents page documents UA expectations + calls out the specific trap
- [ ] Integration test added: pytest that hits the production API with urllib and asserts no 1010

# Related

- Current /api/v1/* is already authenticated; Cloudflare bot protection there is double-coverage
- Affects picortex bulk-upload, future MCP servers, future integrations

title: [Meta] Standing agent-UX research loop: periodic interviews + synthesis + backlog pruning
description:
# Context

Session 2026-04-23 demonstrated a high-value pattern: spawn an independent agent, have it use WikiHub directly, report friction — then file the findings as tickets. That single interview produced 4 substantial tickets (wikihub-855, si1, p26, o6d) and confirmed 1 already-known issue.

# Proposal

Turn this into a standing monthly (or bi-monthly) process:

1. Spawn an agent with a specific persona:
   - \"Publishing agent\" — tries to publish a 50-page markdown tree
   - \"Indexing agent\" — tries to crawl and summarize all public wikis
   - \"Search agent\" — tries to search across wikis and filter results
   - \"Issue-posting agent\" — once wikihub-5b4 ships, try posting issues
2. Give read-only (or scoped write) API key
3. Structured prompt: what works, top friction, missing capabilities, verdict
4. Maintainer reviews findings + files or closes tickets
5. Close the loop: when a reported issue ships, add to a \"fixed from agent feedback\" ledger in the wiki itself

# Why

WikiHub's positioning (\"GitHub for LLM wikis\") means agents ARE the primary user. Optimizing only from human-developer intuition will miss agent-side friction (see the 4 tickets filed 2026-04-23).

# Done when

- [ ] Process documented somewhere persistent (e.g. \`~/code/wikihub/docs/agent-ux-research-process.md\`)
- [ ] First scheduled run on the calendar (cron, or a beads recurring task)
- [ ] Ledger page exists on wikihub itself showing what agents asked for and when it shipped

title: [Agent UX] Idempotency keys on POST /pages (retry safety after 502/429)
description:
# Observed

During picortex bulk-upload, one POST returned 502 (likely transient gateway error). Retrying naively risked creating a duplicate page. The existing 409-on-conflict behavior helps, but doesn't distinguish \"I already succeeded, return the old ID\" from \"someone else created this path, sorry\".

# Proposal

Stripe-style idempotency:

\`\`\`
POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/pages
Idempotency-Key: <uuid-chosen-by-client>
...
\`\`\`

- First call: process normally, store \`(user_id, idempotency_key) → response\` for 24h
- Same key within 24h: return the cached response (same status code, same body)
- Applies to all write endpoints: POST /wikis, POST /pages, PUT /pages/*, bulk endpoints

# Why now

Agents retry aggressively. Without idempotency, 429s or 502s become corruption surfaces (duplicate pages, half-written state). This is cheap to add (a Redis or SQL table keyed on idempotency-key) and makes the whole write surface robust.

# Done when

- [ ] Idempotency-Key header supported on POST /wikis, POST /pages, PUT /pages/*
- [ ] Documented on /agents
- [ ] Integration test: same request twice with same key → same 201 response both times

title: Explore Editorial Picks keep getting clobbered — need data-driven curation
description:
## Problem (recurring)

The Explore page's Editorial Picks section keeps losing its curated wikis. This has happened multiple times. The picks are currently hardcoded in main.py as a list of (owner, slug) tuples, which means any refactor to the explore route can accidentally drop them.

## Current Fix

Hardcoded _EDITORIAL_PICKS list in app/routes/main.py with curated wikis:
- wikihub/wikihub, jacobcole/ideas, jacobcole/quotes, jacobcole/rsi-wiki, jacobcole/admitsphere, jacobcole/llm-wiki-ecosystem, harrisonqian/tools-stack

## Better Long-term Solution

Move editorial picks to a data source (not code):
1. A DB table `editorial_picks` with wiki_id + sort_order
2. Or a config file / admin endpoint to manage picks
3. Or derive from the Featured Wikis page in wikihub/wikihub wiki

This way code refactors can't accidentally drop curated content.

## How to Verify

Visit /explore and check that the "Editorial Picks" section shows multiple wiki cards, not just WikiHub.

The toggle should appear in the nav or settings. Light theme needs a full set of design tokens in system.md.

title: [Agent UX] Auto-linkify URLs in rendered markdown + agent guide: emit explicit [text](url)
description:
# Observed

\`app/renderer.py:208\` initializes markdown-it-py as:
\`\`\`python
md = MarkdownIt(\"commonmark\", {\"html\": False, \"typographer\": True})
\`\`\`
Linkify is NOT enabled. So a bare URL like \`https://example.com\` in a wiki page renders as literal text, not a clickable link. Agents writing content have to remember to wrap every URL as \`[text](url)\` to get clickability.

# Two-pronged fix

## A. Renderer: opt-in autolink at display time

Enable markdown-it-py's linkify plugin:
\`\`\`python
md = MarkdownIt(\"commonmark\", {\"html\": False, \"typographer\": True, \"linkify\": True}).enable('linkify')
\`\`\`

This turns bare \`https://...\` URLs and \`user@host\` emails into links automatically at render time.

- **Per-wiki setting**: \`autolink_urls: true\` (default true for new wikis, false for existing to avoid surprises, OR feature-flag globally for a month then default-on).
- **Scope**: just URLs and emails. Do NOT auto-linkify arbitrary tokens that *might* be slugs — too much false-positive risk.

## B. Agent guide: instruct agents to emit explicit links

Even with autolink enabled, agents authoring content should prefer explicit \`[descriptive text](url)\` over bare URLs because:
- Descriptive anchor text is better for readers and for search
- It's robust if autolink is disabled
- It's the markdown convention

### Where this doc lives

- **\`/agents\`** page on wikihub — add a \"Writing content\" section with conventions
- **\`AGENTS.md\`** in each wiki's git repo (scaffolded default) — per-wiki convention
- **New: \`~/.claude/skills/wikihub/\`** skill — a Claude skill that preloads wikihub-authoring conventions (API, naming, links, wikilinks, frontmatter visibility). Optional but aligned with the \"skills\" theme in Jacob's global config.

### Conventions to document

- Always wrap URLs as \`[descriptive text](url)\` even if autolink is on
- Use \`[[Page Name]]\` wikilinks for intra-wiki references (not URL paths)
- For intra-folder assets, use relative paths: \`[mockup](../mockups/00.html)\`
- Headings should link to authoritative source via a footer row, not inline in the title
- Prefer \`>\` blockquotes for \"what/why\" callouts over inline parentheticals

## Done when

- [ ] Per-wiki \`autolink_urls\` setting in DB + UI toggle
- [ ] Renderer respects setting; default behavior picked (see \"Scope\" above)
- [ ] \`/agents\` page updated with \"Writing content\" section including linkification guidance
- [ ] Scaffolded \`AGENTS.md\` in new wikis includes the convention snippet
- [ ] (Optional, separate sub-task) \`~/.claude/skills/wikihub/\` skill created with API + conventions
- [ ] e2e test: a page with bare URL renders as clickable link when setting is on

## Explicit non-goals for this ticket

- Auto-linkify arbitrary non-URL tokens (e.g. turn every mention of \"picortex\" into a link to that wiki). Too many false positives; separate feature if ever.
- Auto-wikilink-ification (turn \"Foo Bar\" into \`[[Foo Bar]]\` if a page exists). Mentioned in Jacob's notes but deferred — it's a bigger design question about content mutation.

## Related

- wikihub-8vk (Content-Type for non-md) — separate concern
- wikihub previous ticket on Karpathy-style import (wikihub-nt6) — touches similar renderer area
- Touches: app/renderer.py, app/models.py (new Wiki.autolink_urls column), app/templates/settings.html, app/templates/agents.html

title: Edit button missing for unauthenticated users on public-edit pages
description:
Pages with visibility 'public-edit' show the amber 'Public Edit' badge correctly, but no Edit button appears for unauthenticated visitors. The Jinja elif block at reader.html:666 should render the Edit button when the page is public-edit, but it doesn't produce output on production.

Likely cause: the production deployment is running an older version of reader.html that doesn't include the elif block for public-edit/unlisted-edit. The local codebase has this fix but it hasn't been deployed.

Reproduction: visit https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack — page shows 'Public Edit' badge but no Edit pencil button is visible
close_reason: Superseded by wikihub-euw3.1 — root cause in original description was incorrect (not a stale deploy; template reader.html simply never references user_can_edit). Tracking the fix under the public-edit workflow epic.

title: bulk delete and folder delete
description: cannot delete an entire wiki. cannot bulk delete files. cannot delete a folder. no delete button visible in the file list when browsing a folder. need: delete wiki, delete folder, bulk file delete, delete buttons in list view.

title: Git clone/push fails: pre-receive hook missing Flask module
description: Git operations (clone, push) against wikihub repos fail with a server-side pre-receive hook error. The hook imports Flask but the module isn't available in the hook's Python environment. API works fine — only git operations are broken. Workaround: use REST API instead of git.

title: Mobile: sidebar toggle + basic mobile UX audit
description:
## Problem

The left sidebar is not accessible on mobile. There's no hamburger menu or toggle button to open it. This means mobile users can't navigate between pages within a wiki — they're stuck on whatever page they landed on.

## Scope

1. **Sidebar toggle on mobile** — add a hamburger/menu button that opens the sidebar as an overlay on small screens
2. **Basic mobile UX audit** — verify all core flows work on mobile:
   - Reading pages (text wrapping, tables, code blocks)
   - Navigation (breadcrumbs, sidebar, wikilinks)
   - Profile page layout
   - Search
   - Login/signup forms
   - Creating/editing pages (if logged in)

## Files involved
- `app/templates/reader.html` — has sidebar-overlay infrastructure but may lack a toggle button
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same
- `app/templates/base.html` — global nav bar, may need hamburger
- CSS in each template's style block — media queries for mobile
close_reason: Hamburger menu added to reader, folder, and profile pages. Opens sidebar as fixed overlay on mobile.

close_reason: Implemented in this session
