description: surface 'what links here' backlinks in the page sidebar. data already exists in the wikilinks table — just need to query and render. like Wikipedia's 'what links here' feature.

title: [UX] Embed Noos Universal Feedback Widget on wikihub pages
description:
# Context

Noos already designed and implemented a universal feedback widget:
- Architecture doc: `~/code/noos/docs/UNIVERSAL_FEEDBACK_WIDGET.md`
- Entity-feedback doc: `~/code/noos/docs/ENTITY_FEEDBACK_ARCHITECTURE.md`
- Widget source: `~/code/noos/chrome-extension/noos-feedback.js`
- Server route: `~/code/noos/dist/routes/feedback.js`

Key design points from the architecture doc:
- Embeddable via `<script src="...noos.js">`
- Sets `window.__noosWidgetInstalled = true` so Chrome-extension fallback knows to skip
- API: `GET /api/feedback?url=<canonical-url>` → `{ issues: [], annotations: [], counts: {} }`
- URL normalization: strip UTM, trailing slash, fragments

# Task

Add the widget to wikihub's `app/templates/base.html` so every rendered page gets:
1. A floating feedback button (bottom-right)
2. Click → opens the noos widget keyed on the current page's canonical URL (`https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@<owner>/<slug>/<path>`)
3. Shows existing feedback + lets any authenticated noos user leave feedback

# Open questions

- Host the widget script at `wikihub.globalbr.ai/noos.js` (proxy) or hotlink `globalbr.ai/noos.js`? Hotlink is simpler; proxy avoids cross-origin concerns.
- Opt-in per wiki, or on by default? Suggest: off by default, owner can enable via Settings toggle.
- Should the widget also know about *wikihub* issues (from the other ticket about public issue-posting API)? That would unify feedback/issue surface. Probably yes, long-term — but v1 can just show noos feedback.
- Auth interop: noos widget expects noos SSO cookie. Users logged into wikihub (email + password) won't automatically have a noos session. Need cross-SSO or fall back to "log in with noos" button inside the widget.

# Done when

- [ ] Owner toggle in Settings: "Show feedback widget on pages"
- [ ] `base.html` conditionally includes the noos.js script
- [ ] Widget opens and shows feedback count on reader pages
- [ ] Cross-origin cookie story works (or noos login button renders inside widget)

# Related

- `~/code/noos/docs/UNIVERSAL_FEEDBACK_WIDGET.md` (full design)
- `~/code/noos/chrome-extension/noos-feedback.js` (implementation)
- Jacob's principle: reuse existing noos infrastructure rather than build parallel feedback system

title: Graph coloring: only color by folder when subfolders actually exist
description: When coloring graph nodes by folder, if the wiki has no subfolders (just files at the root level), every single node gets a different color because each file is treated as its own 'folder'. Should only apply folder-based coloring when there are actual subdirectories. If everything is flat at the root, use a single color for all nodes.

title: Design community index wiki (@wikihub as curated meta-index)
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub wiki should serve as a curated community index — an 'awesome list of awesome lists.' User stories:

1. A user creates a great 'Best Body Masters List' wiki. They want it featured.
2. The community wiki has a page like 'Best Fitness Wikis' that links to it with a blurb.
3. A visitor lands on @wikihub and can browse curated categories pointing to the best user wikis.

This is different from /explore (algorithmic, by stars/activity). The community wiki is editorial — someone decides what's featured.

Key questions to resolve:
- Is @wikihub/wikihub the right place for this, or should there be a separate @wikihub/featured wiki?
- How do categories work? Pages in the wiki like 'Best AI Tools Wikis', 'Best Reading Lists'?
- Should featured wikis get a badge/indicator on their own pages?
- Who can edit the community index? Just admins, or suggested-edit PRs from users?
- How does this interplay with the Explore page? Does Explore pull from the community wiki's editorial picks?

For v1: probably just manually curated pages in @wikihub/wikihub that link to user wikis with [[cross-wiki wikilinks]] or plain URLs. The index IS the wiki — no special machinery needed. Editorial curation is just writing markdown.

Reference: ListHub had similar brainstorming about community lists as indexes of other lists.

title: Revert to arbitrary history state
description: Add ability to revert a page or wiki to any previous commit. Agent-first: API endpoint POST /wikis/:owner/:slug/revert with {sha, path?}. Web UI: 'Revert to this version' button on the diff view page. Should create a new commit (not force-push), so the revert itself is tracked in history.

title: Sidebar: distinguish folders from cross-wiki links in navigation
description:
## Problem

When navigating the sidebar, there's no visual distinction between:
1. **Folders** — subfolders within the current wiki (e.g. `ideas/` folder inside `jacobcole/jacobcole`)
2. **Wiki links** — links that take you to a different wiki entirely (e.g. from personal wiki to `jacobcole/ideas`)

Both use the same folder/page icons and indentation. Users can't tell if clicking will keep them in the current wiki or jump to a different one.

Currently the sidebar only shows items from the current wiki's file tree. Cross-wiki links only appear in page content as regular markdown links. The profile page has a separate "Wikis" section, but this pattern isn't used elsewhere.

## Proposed Solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Wiki icon in sidebar (minimal)
Add a third icon type — a small "wiki" icon (e.g. book/globe/grid) — distinct from the folder and page icons. When the sidebar lists sub-wikis (e.g. on a profile or index page), use this icon instead of folder.

### Option B: "Your wikis" sidebar section
Like the profile page already does — show a separate labeled section "WIKIS" below the file tree in the sidebar. This already works on profile.html (lines 176-185). Extend to reader.html and folder.html when viewing a user's personal wiki.

### Option C: Breadcrumb-aware context switching
When you click from one wiki into another, the breadcrumb should make it visually obvious you've changed context — e.g. the sidebar header changes from "JACOB'S IDEAS" to the new wiki's name, with a "← back to profile" link. (This already happens, but the transition is jarring with no warning.)

### Option D: Wiki cards in page content
When the index page links to other wikis, render those as visually distinct cards (title + description + page count) rather than plain text links. This makes it obvious in the content area that these are whole wikis, not pages.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option B** — it's the simplest and profile.html already has the pattern. Then add **Option A** (wiki icon) for polish. Option D is nice for landing pages but is a bigger change.

## Files involved
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` builds the sidebar
- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 181-209)
- `app/templates/folder.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 47-69)
- `app/templates/profile.html` — already has the "Wikis" section pattern (lines 176-185)
close_reason: Implemented Option B + A: sidebar now shows a 'Jacob Cole's Wikis' section with book icons below the current wiki's file tree. Applied to reader.html, folder.html, and profile.html. Deployed to production.

title: TOC double-encodes HTML entities (ampersand shows as &)
description:
The table of contents in the right sidebar double-encodes HTML entities. Headings like 'Homerow & Vimium' render correctly in the main content but show as 'Homerow & Vimium' in the TOC.

Root cause: extract_toc() in renderer.py (line 38) strips HTML tags but doesn't unescape HTML entities. The resulting text (containing &) is then escaped again by Jinja's auto-escaping, producing &amp; in the HTML.

Fix: add html.unescape(text) after the re.sub() call in extract_toc().

Reproduction: visit https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack and look at CONTENTS sidebar — 'Homerow & Vimium' and 'Notes & Knowledge'
close_reason: Duplicate of wikihub-osd (same TOC double-encoding bug)

title: Clean up junk/test accounts polluting production People page
description: Multiple test accounts are visible on production People page: stress-test-1, stresstest123, stresstest_probe_99, ratelimit-test-1 through ratelimit-test-5, 'test spaces', 'test<script>...', test_upper, '../../../etc/passwd'. Need DB cleanup and possibly an admin delete-account endpoint.

title: Public-edit workflow: fix end-to-end UX gaps
description:
Umbrella ticket tracking the audit findings for the public-edit visibility workflow. Anonymous and non-owner users who technically have write permission via public-edit cannot discover or invoke editing from the UI in multiple places — the backend ACL (app/acl.py:125 can_write returns True for public-edit) is correct, but templates don't honor the user_can_edit flag that wiki.py:916 already computes.

## Scope

- Fix reader.html to use user_can_edit (supersedes wikihub-bvl)
- Make wiki_index compute and pass user_can_edit to reader.html
- Surface editing affordances in folder.html for non-owners
- Unify sidebar New page link under can_write, not is_owner
- Surface History in the page-actions bar (currently URL-only)
- Author attribution: anonymous commit fingerprint for revert/moderation
- "Request edit access" / "Sign in to edit" CTA for view-only pages

Related existing tickets:
- wikihub-bvl (covered by child A below; description root cause is wrong — not a stale deploy, template never reads user_can_edit)
- wikihub-4t2 (display-layer rename partly done in reader.html:274, storage rename still needed)
- wikihub-ub2.5 (panic button) — pairs with fingerprint work
- wikihub-ub2.6 (revert N anonymous edits) — depends on fingerprint ticket below

## Files (verified via audit)
- app/routes/wiki.py:777 wiki_page, :916 user_can_edit, :948 edit_page, :997 anon author attribution
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322 page-actions bar (never references user_can_edit)
- app/templates/folder.html:108-111, 181-186 owner-only gates
- app/acl.py:106 can_read, :125 can_write

title: Fix Edit button visibility: reader.html must honor user_can_edit for non-owners and anonymous users
description:
The variable user_can_edit is computed correctly in app/routes/wiki.py:916 (is_owner or can_write(...)) and passed to reader.html — but the template never reads it. The page-actions block at reader.html:295-322 only has branches for is_owner (line 295) and authenticated non-owner (line 306, Star/Fork only). Anonymous visitors and non-owner ACL grantees get no Edit button even when entitled.

## Fix
Replace or extend the conditional at reader.html:295 with a branch that renders the Edit button whenever user_can_edit is true, independent of is_owner and is_authenticated.

## Supersedes
wikihub-bvl — that ticket's stated cause (stale deployment) is incorrect; the bug is also present in the local repo. Close bvl when this lands.

## Reproduction
https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack (public-edit page). Amber "Public Edit" badge shows, no Edit button.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322
- app/routes/wiki.py:916 (logic is correct, just unused)
close_reason: Closed

title: wiki_index route must compute and pass user_can_edit to reader.html
description:
When a wiki has an index.md, the wiki_index route renders reader.html but does not compute user_can_edit (app/routes/wiki.py:594, rendering at :632). Even after the reader.html fix lands, users on a public-edit wiki's index page get no Edit button because the template receives no truthy user_can_edit.

## Fix
Mirror the user_can_edit computation from wiki_page (wiki.py:916) in wiki_index and include it in the template context.

## Files
- app/routes/wiki.py:594 wiki_index
close_reason: Closed

title: Folder view: show New page / New folder for non-owners on public-edit wikis
description:
folder.html gates New page / New folder links (lines 108-111 and 181-186) on current_user.id == wiki.owner_id. On a public-edit wiki, a non-owner or anonymous visitor cannot discover how to create content from the folder view, even though the backend new_page route (app/routes/wiki.py:1024) accepts anonymous POSTs.

## Fix
Replace the owner-only guard with a can_write check. Pass user_can_edit from the folder route into the template and reuse it.

## Files
- app/templates/folder.html:108-111, 181-186
- app/routes/wiki.py folder route (look for folder_view / similar)

title: Sidebar 'New page' link should follow can_write, not is_owner
description:
Sidebar in reader.html:227-239 gates the New page link on current_user.id == wiki.owner_id. Should show whenever user_can_edit (or a wiki-level can_write) is true. Matches the folder view fix above.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:227-239

title: Surface page History in the page-actions bar
description:
The history routes (/@owner/wiki/history at wiki.py:709, /@owner/wiki/page/history at :719) exist but are not linked from any page surface. Users must guess the URL.

## Fix
Add a History button to the page-actions bar in reader.html next to Edit, linking to /@owner/wiki/page/history. Visible to any viewer for public pages; owner-only can be the conservative first cut.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322 page-actions bar

title: Anonymous editor fingerprint: record IP/session in git commit trailer
description:
Anonymous edits currently all commit as 'anonymous <anon@wikihub>' (app/routes/wiki.py:997-998, app/git_sync.py:149-158) with no IP, session, or fingerprint. The git log cannot distinguish one anonymous editor from another. This blocks any meaningful anonymous moderation.

## Fix
Add a git commit trailer (e.g. 'X-Wikihub-Session: <hash>' where hash = sha256(ip + daily_salt)) on anonymous commits. Keep the visible author as 'anonymous' for privacy; the trailer gives admins a revert-by-session primitive without exposing raw IPs in history.

## Unblocks
- wikihub-ub2.6 (revert last N anonymous edits) — currently nonfunctional as a useful tool because all anonymous editors look identical in the git log
- Pairs with wikihub-ub2.5 (panic button) and wikihub-ub2.1 (IP rate limit)

## Files
- app/routes/wiki.py:997-998
- app/git_sync.py:149-158

title: Request edit access / Sign in to edit: contextual CTA on view-only and restricted pages
description:
Today a visitor to a public (view-only) page sees only the badge and download menu — no invitation to request access or sign in. On a public-edit page where anonymous writes are disabled (per wikihub-ub2.5 panic button), the visitor sees nothing prompting them to sign in either.

## Minimal standard implementation

Add a 'Request edit access' button to the page-actions bar when:
- user is not owner AND user_can_edit is false AND visibility in ('public', 'private')

On click, the minimal first cut is one of:
1. mailto: link with prefilled subject and body addressed to the wiki owner's public email (zero new infra)
2. POST to a tiny /api/v1/request-access endpoint that creates a notification row for the owner (nicer UX, one model + one route)

Also add a 'Sign in to edit' affordance when:
- user is anonymous AND the page is public-edit AND anonymous writes are disabled for this wiki

## Why standard / not overthought

- No new ACL machinery; no pending-approval queues; no invitation tokens in v1
- Owner sees the request in existing notification channel
- Can evolve into a real approvals queue later

## Relation
- Rides on the unified toolbar (wikihub-zy0h)
- Pairs naturally with anonymous-writes-disabled panic button (wikihub-ub2.5)

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html page-actions bar
- possibly new: app/routes/access.py with POST /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/request-access

title: One-click navigation back to own profile from any page
description: Currently getting back to your own profile requires opening the account dropdown menu then clicking 'View profile' — two clicks. Should be one click from anywhere. Options: (1) clicking the avatar/initial in the nav goes directly to profile, (2) add a persistent 'Home' link in the nav, (3) the [[wikihub]] logo could go to your profile when logged in instead of the landing page. Need to decide which pattern fits best.
close_reason: Avatar in nav bar is now a direct link to profile (one click), dropdown chevron separated

title: Restructure Jeremy Nixon wiki into Karpathy three-layer format
description:
Jeremy's 452 pages are currently flat in the wiki root. The Karpathy pattern organizes into:

- schema.md — what this wiki is about, how it's structured
- index.md — table of contents (already exists)
- log.md — timeline of additions
- wiki/ — compiled, curated knowledge pages by topic
- raw/ — unprocessed source entries (the current 450 dated posts)

An agent could:
1. Move all dated entries into raw/
2. Create wiki/ pages that compile the best insights by topic (machine intelligence, learning, startups, thinking frameworks, etc.)
3. Generate schema.md describing the wiki structure
4. Create log.md with a timeline

This is a good showcase of the 'agent maintains a wiki' use case — the raw material exists, an LLM compiles it into structured knowledge.

title: Per-person sharing: wiki settings page with grant management
Graph system: page graph, full graph, filtered graph with progressive coloring
description:
## Context

Wikis are graphs. The wikilink structure already contains rich relationship data — we just need to visualize it. This spec defines a three-view graph system that works on any wiki (structure-agnostic) but looks better with metadata (progressive enhancement).

## Three views, one graph engine

Underlying data: nodes = pages, edges = wikilinks. Derived from the wikilinks index wikihub already builds for backlinks. No separate graph database needed.

### 1. Page graph (every page sidebar/footer)
- Current page = center node, highlighted
- Show all pages directly linked TO or FROM this page (1-hop)
- Optionally expand to 2-hop (friends of friends)
- Click node → navigate to that page
- Like Obsidian's local graph — scoped, useful, not overwhelming
- Must be fast enough to render inline on every page load

### 2. Full graph (wiki homepage + dedicated route)
- Every page is a node, every wikilink is an edge
- Force-directed layout — clusters emerge naturally from link density
- The "wow" view showing the shape of the whole knowledge base
- Zoom, pan, search, click-to-navigate
- Shown on wiki homepage (index.md) as an interactive embed and at a dedicated `/@user/wiki/graph` route

### 3. Filtered graph (overlays on full graph)
- Filter by: directory path, frontmatter `type:`, tag, or search query
- "Show only pages connected to X" — page graph launched from full graph
- "Hide all conversation pages" — reduce noise to see topic structure
- Toggle filters on/off, combine them

## Coloring strategy (progressive enhancement)

The graph looks good with zero schema knowledge and great with it:

| Signal | How to use | Agnostic? |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Directory path | `wiki/courses/` = blue, `wiki/topics/` = green, etc. | Yes — any wiki with folders |
| Frontmatter `type:` | Override directory color if present, more precise | Semi — only typed wikis |
| Frontmatter `tags:` | Secondary color ring or icon badge | Semi |
| Link count | Node size scales with inbound + outbound links | Yes — derived from wikilinks |
| Recency | Opacity or glow based on `updated:` or git date | Semi |

**Fallback:** no directories, no frontmatter, no nothing → every node same color, sized by link count. Still works. Degrades gracefully.

**Key rule:** never require metadata for the graph to function. Only use it to enhance when present.

## Where graphs appear

- **Page sidebar/footer**: page graph (1-hop), always visible, lightweight
- **Wiki homepage (index.md)**: full graph embedded inline — interactive `<canvas>` or collapsible section. WikiHub auto-injects on index.md pages, or expose a `{{graph}}` embed directive
- **Dedicated `/graph` route**: full-screen interactive graph with filter controls, per wiki

The homepage embed doesn't violate structure-agnosticism — it's a rendering feature of the homepage route, not imposed on content.

## Tech notes

- **Data source:** wikilinks index (already exists for backlinks). Endpoints: `GET /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph` returning `{nodes: [{id, title, type?, dir?}], edges: [{source, target}]}`
- **Frontend:** d3-force or similar force-directed layout. Lightweight, browser-native.
- **Performance:** wikis under 500 pages — render all nodes. Above that — progressive loading or cluster-first-then-expand. Page graph (1-hop) must be instant.
- **Interaction:** hover = show title + link count, click = navigate, drag = reposition, scroll = zoom

title: Scoped/restricted API keys: tiered child keys for agents in different trust contexts
description:
Original scope: tiered child API keys for agents in different trust contexts.

REFERENCED BY: picortex-ybz — picortex wants to mint per-chat child keys so each group chat the bot joins gets scoped wikihub access (namespace = chats/<chat_id>, read: public, write: chats/<chat_id>, no delete). picortex-ybz is blocked on this ticket landing.

Core API needed:
- POST /api/keys/derive — input: parent key + scope object {namespace, read[], write[], delete[], expires_in_days}. Output: child key string.
- DELETE /api/keys/<child_key_id> — revoke
- Every call made with a child key audit-logs the child_key_id + parent_key_id

This is still P1 because it unlocks the whole delegated-agent-access pattern, not just picortex.
notes:
## Decisions (Jacob, 2026-04-23)

1. **No path globs.** Out of scope, not just v1 — keep the model simple. Visibility ceiling + wiki allowlist/denylist is the entire scope vocabulary.

2. **No grandchildren.** Only top-level (parent) keys can mint scoped keys. Scoped keys cannot mint further scoped keys.

3. **NEW REQUIREMENT — visible session indication.** When a scoped key is used to authenticate a browser session (via API-key→session swap or magic-link with scoped credentials), the UI must visibly indicate the session is restricted. Concretely:
   - Persistent banner or badge in the chrome (header) showing the scope label and key restriction summary, e.g.: `🔒 Restricted access: "group-chat-friends" — read-only, public wikis only`
   - Color-coded so a human can tell at a glance they are NOT in their main session
   - Recommend: amber/orange banner band along the top, since the design system already uses amber as the accent color
   - Should also gate or label dangerous-looking UI affordances (delete buttons etc.) with the scope info

   Why: prevents a human from confusing a scoped session for their main one and being surprised when actions are denied or content is missing.

## Re-confirmed framing

The mental model is just: **"main key = full access; other keys = restricted access to the same account."** Everything else (the API surface, scope dimensions, audit log, settings UI) follows from that.

title: Connection system: cross-referencing patterns that prevent noise
description:
## Context

Cross-references are the whole point of a wiki over flat notes. But random/spurious connections make it worse than no connections. This issue defines a connection system that maximizes signal and minimizes noise.

Tested on a 115-page wiki — the patterns below emerged from what worked and what didn't.

## The problem

Agents generating wiki pages tend to either:
- Add zero connections (each page is an island)
- Add random connections based on keyword overlap ("both mention 'learning'" → spurious link)

Both are bad. We need inline wikilinks woven naturally into prose — like Wikipedia, not like a "see also" appendix.

## How connections work: inline wikilinks in prose

Links are woven into the text naturally as the page is written:

> "Harrison worked at [[orbit-internship|Orbit]] where [[mark-khrapko|Mark Khrapko]] mentored him on [[hiring-and-teams|hiring philosophy]] and he developed the [[cnn-spectrogram-analysis|CNN spectrogram classifier]]"

No dedicated "connections" section. If a relationship matters, it belongs in the prose. If it's not worth mentioning in prose, it's not a real connection. Backlinks (auto-generated by renderer) handle the reverse direction.

### Rules for inline linking

- **Link on first mention only** — don't link the same page multiple times in one section
- **Use display text** — `[[slug|Natural Text]]` so prose reads naturally
- **Don't over-link** — link when it adds navigational value, not every noun
- **Don't self-link** — a page never links to itself
- **Conservative matching** — better to miss a link than create a wrong one. "Mark" alone is ambiguous; "Mark Khrapko" is not.

Agents creating wiki pages should include inline links AS THEY WRITE, not as a separate pass. The schema/CLAUDE.md should specify this behavior.

## Cross-section connection taxonomy

Agents should specifically look for these high-value connection types:
- **person ↔ topic**: conversation pages link to topics discussed with that person
- **course ↔ topic**: academic material links to personal exploration of same concepts
- **topic ↔ topic**: thematic overlap between different knowledge areas
- **experience ↔ reflection**: what happened links to what was learned from it

Cross-section connections are almost always more valuable than within-section.

## Quality rules (anti-spam)

**MUST:**
- Pass the test: "would a reader learn something by following this link?"
- Reference specific shared content, not just shared keywords

**SHOULD:**
- Every page should have at least 2 outbound inline links (prevents orphans)
- Inline links are uncapped but governed by "first mention, don't over-link"

**MUST NOT:**
- Link based solely on keyword overlap without semantic relationship
- Create a dedicated "connections" or "see also" section — if it's not in the prose, it's not a connection
- Link every page in the same category to each other

## Lint-based link discovery

Automated agent pass for existing wikis that lack inline links:
1. Build alias map: page slug → matching text patterns (full name, course code, key phrase)
2. Scan prose for mentions of other pages
3. Replace first occurrence with `[[slug|matched text]]`
4. Conservative: skip ambiguous matches
5. Flag pages with 0-1 outbound links for review

## Renderer support

- **Inline links**: render `[[slug|Display Text]]` as clickable links showing only display text
- **Backlinks panel**: auto-generated list of pages that link TO this page — this replaces any need for a "connections" section
- Optional: mini knowledge graph showing 1-hop connections from current page

title: Loose vertical rhythm on rendered wiki pages (line-height, p/h2 margins)
description:
Page URL: https://jacobcole.wikihub.md/picortex/docs/mockups/

The spacing on this page looks ugly. Need to investigate:
- What CSS rules control spacing on mockup/docs pages
- Whether images/mockups have incorrect margins or padding
- Whether list/heading spacing is off
- Compare with other wiki pages that look fine

Use Chrome DevTools MCP to inspect the rendered page non-invasively.
notes:
## Correction — original diagnosis was wrong
The initial investigation targeted the folder-index page at /picortex/docs/mockups/ (empty space below single card). The actual complaint is about vertical spacing **within rendered content pages** (paragraphs, table rows, list items, headings too far apart).

## Real diagnosis (measured on live page, template `main.content-wrap`)

| Element | Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| content-wrap line-height | 1.6 (25.6px/16px) | ok |
| p line-height | **1.75** (26.25px/15px) | too loose — use 1.5–1.6 |
| p margin-bottom | 18px | slightly loose |
| h2 margin-top | **40px** | very loose, creates big section gaps |
| h2 margin-bottom | 16px + 8px padding-bottom | | 
| li margin-bottom | 4px | ok |

Consecutive-paragraph gaps observed: 18, 100, 153, 203, 378 — the big ones are headings between stacking their 40px top margin plus their own height.

In tables (visible in the user's screenshot), `td` inherits the 1.75 line-height, so 2-line cells balloon to ~48-50px row height.

## Suggested fixes (cheapest first)
1. `.content-wrap { line-height: 1.55 }` (or target `p, li, td`) — biggest single win
2. `p { margin-bottom: 12px }` (from 18)
3. `h2 { margin-top: 28px }` (from 40); drop padding-bottom to 4px
4. `td { line-height: 1.45 }` + tighten td padding

## Where to look
Search the wikihub template/CSS for: `.content-wrap`, paragraph styles, table styles. The folder-index layout issue (my original diagnosis) is a separate, real bug but lower priority — could be split into its own ticket if desired.

title: WikiHub PKM client app (desktop + mobile)
description:
## Vision

Build a dedicated client app for WikiHub that turns it into a full personal knowledge management (PKM) system — like Obsidian but backed by WikiHub's hosted git repos.

## Platforms

- Desktop: Electron app (or native Swift for Mac)
- Mobile: iOS / Android
- Web: could be the first step (online editor/reader)

## Key Questions to Decide

1. **Build from scratch vs. extend existing?**
   - Build from scratch — most control, cleanest WikiHub integration, but most work
   - Fork/extend an open-source markdown editor (e.g., Marktext, Nota, Obsidian-like)
   - Integrate with Notestream (IdeaFlow's existing app)
   - Wrap the WikiHub web UI in Electron as a starting point

2. **Offline-first architecture?**
   - Local git clone of wiki repos + sync on connect
   - Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins? merge?)
   - How much works without internet?

3. **What differentiates this from Obsidian?**
   - WikiHub-native: publish, fork, star, discover — social PKM
   - Agent-native: MCP server means your AI assistant can maintain your wiki
   - Karpathy pattern built in: raw/, wiki/, schema structure
   - No plugin ecosystem needed — opinionated defaults

4. **MVP scope**
   - Online web editor might be the fastest first step
   - WikiHub already renders markdown — adding an edit mode could be v0.1
   - Desktop app (Electron) as v0.2
   - Mobile as v0.3

## Why

Using WikiHub as a PKM system requires a better editing experience than the current web form. A dedicated client would make it viable for daily use — quick capture, wikilink navigation, offline editing, keyboard shortcuts, graph view, etc.

title: Build Karpathy import tool: auto-generate log.md + schema.md from imported wikis
description:
When importing external content (blogs, Google Sites, GitHub Pages) into WikiHub, automatically generate:

1. **log.md** — retrospective timeline reconstructed from dates in filenames/content. Each dated entry gets a log line: '## 2017-02-01 — Added: Ways to Discover Contrarian Truths'. This creates the Karpathy activity feed pattern even for imported content.

2. **schema.md** — auto-generated from analyzing the content structure. Describes what the wiki covers, how it's organized, and instructions for an agent maintaining it.

3. **Ripple updates** — when a new raw source is ingested, the agent should update relevant wiki/ compiled pages AND append to log.md.

The key Karpathy mechanic: 'Ingesting one source can update 10-15 wiki pages.' The schema tells the agent HOW to do this. log.md records THAT it happened.

This should be a reusable tool/skill, not one-off code. Every wiki import should produce this structure automatically.

Reference: Karpathy gist https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f
See also: memory/reference_llmwiki.md for the three-layer pattern.

title: [Agent UX] API discoverability: /api/v1/openapi.json + X-Request-ID + consistent error envelopes
description:
# Observed

Independent agent review 2026-04-23 noted three inter-related discoverability gaps:

## 1. No OpenAPI schema

\`GET /api/v1/openapi.json\` → 404. Discovery requires reading three separate places with minor drift:
- \`/agents\` HTML page
- \`/llms.txt\` root file
- \`app/routes/api*.py\` source

**Fix:** use \`flask-smorest\` or \`apispec\` to auto-generate OpenAPI 3.1 from the Flask route defs. Publish at \`/api/v1/openapi.json\`. Link from \`/agents\` and \`/llms.txt\`.

Bonus: agent clients (OpenAI function-calling, Anthropic tools) can consume OpenAPI and autogenerate tool schemas.

## 2. No X-Request-ID on responses

When something fails — especially the \`/history\` 500 — agents have no request-ID to quote when filing a bug. Server logs and client error get disconnected.

**Fix:** add Flask middleware that generates a uuid per request, echoes on response header \`X-Request-ID\`, and tags every log line.

## 3. Inconsistent error envelopes

Observed:
- 404 from API routes → JSON \`{error, message}\` ✅
- 405 Method Not Allowed → Flask default HTML ❌
- 500 from broken /history → JSON but zero diagnostic ❌
- Cloudflare 1010 → HTML block page ❌ (separate issue wikihub-8dz)

**Fix:** register a global Flask errorhandler for 4xx/5xx on \`/api/*\` blueprints that always returns \`{error, message, request_id}\` JSON. Ensure Cloudflare bypasses /api/* for bot-fight (from wikihub-8dz).

# Done when

- [ ] /api/v1/openapi.json live and linked
- [ ] X-Request-ID echoed on every response
- [ ] All /api/* errors return JSON envelope

title: [Agent UX] Read-side API completeness: list-own-wikis, README↔index alias, HEAD, bulk-read
description:
# Observed

Independent agent review 2026-04-23 found several gaps in the READ-side API:

## 1. No way to list my own wikis

Given only an API key, an agent cannot discover which wikis it can write to:
- \`GET /api/v1/wikis\` → 405 Method Not Allowed
- \`GET /api/v1/users/<name>/wikis\` → 404
- Must scrape \`/@user\` HTML to enumerate

**Fix:** \`GET /api/v1/wikis?owner=me\` (or \`?owner=<username>\`) returning the same shape as the single-wiki GET.

## 2. No README.md → index.md alias

Agents from a GitHub mental model call \`GET /api/v1/wikis/.../pages/README.md\` expecting the landing page. Returns 404. The canonical path is \`index.md\`.

**Fix:** treat \`README.md\` and \`README\` as aliases for \`index.md\`. OR document prominently.

## 3. No HEAD /pages/<path>

Agents can't check staleness without pulling full content. A \`HEAD\` returning \`ETag\` + \`Last-Modified\` would let them skip big reads when their cache is fresh.

**Fix:** wire HEAD to the same handler as GET but return headers only.

## 4. No bulk-read endpoint (counterpart to the bulk-write ticket wikihub-2zh)

Reading a whole wiki means N round-trips. For agents doing RAG or wiki-mirroring, this is O(N) latency.

**Fix:** \`GET /api/v1/wikis/<o>/<s>/bulk-read?paths=a.md,b.md\` or NDJSON stream. Workaround exists today via \`/@o/s/llms-full.txt\` but that's HTML-oriented, not REST.

# Done when

- [ ] list-my-wikis endpoint
- [ ] README↔index alias (or clear doc)
- [ ] HEAD /pages/<path>
- [ ] bulk-read JSON endpoint

title: [Agent UX] default_visibility param on POST /wikis + POST /wikis scaffolding improvements
description:
# Observed

POST /api/v1/wikis with \"template\":\"structured\" creates index.md, log.md, schema.md as PRIVATE by default. I wanted them public for the picortex wiki, so after creation I had to call /bulk-visibility to flip them. Two write calls instead of one, and I needed to know the scaffolded paths ahead of time to flip them.

# Proposal

1. Accept \`default_visibility\` in POST /wikis:
\`\`\`json
{
  \"slug\": \"picortex\",
  \"title\": \"...\",
  \"template\": \"structured\",
  \"default_visibility\": \"public\"
}
\`\`\`
Apply to all scaffolded pages. Default remains \"private\" for backwards compat.

2. Return the scaffolded page list in the POST /wikis response:
\`\`\`json
{
  \"id\": 149, \"slug\": \"picortex\", ...,
  \"scaffolded_pages\": [
    {\"path\": \"index.md\", \"visibility\": \"public\"},
    {\"path\": \"log.md\", \"visibility\": \"public\"},
    {\"path\": \"schema.md\", \"visibility\": \"public\"}
  ]
}
\`\`\`

Agents don't have to guess what got created.

# Done when

- [ ] default_visibility accepted and applied
- [ ] scaffolded_pages returned in POST /wikis response
- [ ] /agents page shows an example

title: KaTeX does not render LaTeX align/paren delimiters
description: Pages using standard LaTeX delimiters like \begin{align}...\end{align} and \(...\) show raw LaTeX source instead of rendered math. The KaTeX auto-render config only includes dollar-sign delimiters. Verified on @jemoka/knowledge-base/raw/concept/kbhbayes_theorem which has Bayes theorem formulas displayed as plain text. Additionally the markdown renderer appears to strip backslashes before parentheses, converting \( to ( before KaTeX can process them.

title: recently updated feed on wiki homepage and profiles
description: show last N modified pages on wiki homepage and user profiles. data already available from git history. render as a 'recently updated' section like Farzapedia's homepage sidebar.

title: Polish people cards with visibility badges and richer profile metadata
description: Follow up on the first-pass people browsing feature by adding spec-consistent visibility badges on explore/profile cards, richer personal-wiki metadata on person cards, and finalizing mobile polish for the person-page sidebar.

title: Add one-click magic-link sign-in for agents
description: Let an authenticated agent generate a short-lived one-click sign-in link for the account owner, so the browser session can be established without typing credentials or pasting API keys. Update the spec and add e2e coverage.
acceptance_criteria: API endpoint returns a one-click login URL for the authenticated account; visiting the link logs the user into a browser session and invalidates the token; link expires; spec updated; e2e coverage added.
notes: Implement short-lived single-use magic login links for agent-generated browser sign-in, update spec, and add e2e coverage.
close_reason: Implemented short-lived single-use magic sign-in links via API and browser route, documented the agent flow, updated the spec, and added e2e coverage.

title: article type system with per-type rendering (person, book, place)
description: add a type: frontmatter key (person, book, place, artifact, assessment) with per-type rendering. person gets infobox, book gets cover, place gets map link. builds on infobox feature. inspired by Farzapedia's category system.

title: Editor preview tab shows plaintext instead of rendered markdown
description: The Preview tab in the editor (editor.html) shows raw markdown text via textContent, not rendered output. Needs either a client-side markdown renderer or an AJAX call to the server's render endpoint. Related to wikihub-18e (editor QA). This is the biggest UX gap in the editing experience.

title: Account dropdown menu disappears before clicking menu items
description: The account dropdown menu in the nav only works if you click the avatar icon. Hovering opens it but moving the mouse down to the menu items causes it to disappear before you can click them. Likely a gap between the trigger and the dropdown that breaks the :hover chain, or the mouseenter/mouseleave handlers are too aggressive. Needs a hover delay or bridge element.

title: Public edit (public-edit visibility) not working from web UI
description: Pages with public-edit visibility should be editable by any logged-in user, not just the owner. The web UI edit button may not appear or the route may reject non-owners even when the page allows public editing. Confirmed: API correctly rejects non-owner edits on non-public-edit pages. Need to verify the public-edit flow works end-to-end.

title: Editor route crashes on production: llm_api_key_encrypted column missing
description:
Navigating to any /edit page on production (e.g. https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack/index/edit) throws a 500 error:

sqlalchemy.exc.ProgrammingError: (psycopg2.errors.UndefinedColumn) column users.llm_api_key_encrypted does not exist

The code references a column that hasn't been added to the production database schema. A migration is needed to add this column.

Screenshot: /tmp/stress_edit_17_editor.png

title: Use underscores for wiki page URLs
description: Canonical page URLs should use underscores instead of spaces; accept underscores in routing/API; redirect space/%20 URLs to underscore form; update URL generation and wikilink rendering.
close_reason: Switched page URL generation to underscores, added URL decode for underscores, canonical redirect for space URLs, updated wikilinks/templates/API/agent surfaces, and verified via tests + browser.

title: private wikis leak page existence and index content to unauthenticated users
description:
Two related access control issues with private wikis:

1. **Index page visible on private wikis** — if a wiki is fully private, visiting the wiki index still renders the page. It should deny access entirely (redirect to login or show a 'no access' message).

2. **Direct links to private pages show empty page shell** — if you have a direct link to a private page (e.g. from a search engine or shared URL), the route renders the page template with no content instead of showing a clear 'no access' or 'this page is private' message. This leaks that the page exists and looks like a bug to the user.

Expected behavior:
- Private wiki index: 403/404 or redirect, not a rendered page
- Direct link to private page: clear 'no access' message, not an empty page shell

title: [Agent UX][BUG] Search scoping silently broken + list-pages ignores limit/offset
description:
# Two distinct bugs found by an independent agent review 2026-04-23

## 1. Search ?wiki= param silently ignored without ?scope=wiki

Calling \`GET /api/v1/search?q=picortex&wiki=jacobcole/picortex\` returns global results (including \`harrisonqian/awesome\`), NOT just picortex pages. The combo \`?scope=wiki&wiki=owner/slug\` works, but:
- Neither \`/agents\` nor \`/llms.txt\` documents that scope=wiki is required
- Sending \`wiki=...\` without \`scope=...\` fails silently instead of erroring
- Agents that wrote 'scope search to one wiki' code from the README will ship buggy scoping

Source: `app/routes/api_wikis.py:1068` shows the scope= gate.

### Fix
- **Error loudly** when \`wiki=\` is set but \`scope=wiki\` is not (HTTP 400 with \"hint: add scope=wiki\")
- OR **treat \`wiki=\` as implicit scope** (more DWIM)
- Update \`/agents\` and \`/llms.txt\` either way

## 2. List-pages endpoint ignores ?limit and ?offset

\`GET /api/v1/wikis/<o>/<s>/pages?limit=5\` returns all pages (no pagination applied). Route at `app/routes/api_wikis.py:446` has no pagination logic. Fine at 35 pages; catastrophic at 3,500.

### Fix
- Implement \`limit\` (default 100, max 1000) + \`offset\` or cursor-based pagination
- Return \`next\` / \`has_more\` fields in the response envelope
- Integration test

# Done when

- [ ] Search scope validation + docs
- [ ] Pagination on list-pages + response envelope updated

title: Simplify account trigger to avatar-only in top nav
description: Remove the inline @username label from the authenticated account trigger in the top-right nav while keeping the username visible in the dropdown summary.
acceptance_criteria: Top nav account trigger shows avatar affordance without inline username text; dropdown still shows display name and @username.
close_reason: Removed inline username from top-nav account trigger, kept identity visible in dropdown, verified with full e2e suite, and pushed.

title: Per-line sharing: extend private bands with @user grants
description: Extend private bands (`<\!-- private -->`) to support per-user visibility: `<\!-- private @alice @bob -->`. Content inside these bands would be stripped for all users except those named. This is a natural extension of both the private band system and the ACL grant system — bands control inline visibility, ACL controls page/folder/wiki visibility. Implementation would need: (1) extend _scan_private_markers to parse usernames from opening tag, (2) extend strip_private_bands to accept a user parameter and only strip bands the user isn't granted, (3) update the renderer pipeline to pass current user through, (4) update public mirror generation to fully strip all user-scoped private bands. Deferred — build after page/folder/wiki-level sharing is solid.

title: link hover preview cards (LessWrong-style)
description: when hovering over a wikilink or internal link, show a popover card with a rendered snippet of the target page. cards should be approximately square, show the first ~paragraph of content with a (read more) link, and stack when multiple links are hovered in sequence (like LessWrong). should work for both [[wikilinks]] and regular markdown links to wiki pages. reference: LessWrong's hover preview system.

title: Profile vs personal wiki: clicking a person's name should go to /@username, not /@username/username
description:
On the Explore page, wiki cards for personal wikis (where slug == username) link to /@username/username (the wiki root), not /@username (the profile). This is confusing because:

1. The profile page IS the personal wiki rendered — they show the same content
2. But the profile has the user header, wiki sidebar with project wikis, and 'Wikis' section
3. The wiki root is just the wiki content without the profile chrome

For personal wikis, links should go to /@username (the profile) everywhere:
- Explore page wiki cards
- Search results
- Any place that shows a personal wiki as a card/link

For project wikis (slug != username), links should go to /@username/slug as normal.

The deeper question: should there even be a difference between /@username and /@username/username? Currently the profile page renders the personal wiki's index.md as its main content. Maybe /@username/username should just redirect to /@username. That would make the personal wiki's web identity always be the profile URL, while git clone still uses /@username/username.git.
close_reason: Personal wiki links go to profile. /@user/user redirects 302 to /@user.

title: v2 milestone
description: Core platform features: abuse protection, social features, content import, polish.

title: IP rate limit on writes (10/min/IP)
description: Anti-abuse priority #1. flask-limiter already referenced in app/__init__.py. Decorator config on write endpoints. Infra-level, not moderation.

title: Comments on pages and wikis
description: ACL vocabulary already reserves 'comment' role. Need Comment model, API endpoints, UI component. Social feature that drives engagement.

title: Comments MVP design: inline block-level vs page-level threads, ACL, rendering
description:
Design doc / decision ticket — must land before the code tickets. Answer these questions and commit the decisions to docs/comments-design.md:

## Key decisions
1. Scope: page-level threads only (v1) vs inline-block (attach to a heading/paragraph) vs both
2. Storage: new Comment model in SQLAlchemy vs git-committed comments.md-style sidecar (git-native is cute but harder to moderate)
3. ACL: who can comment — reuse the 'comment' role reserved in app/acl.py (line ~15, VALID_ROLES); map visibility → default comment permission (public = anyone incl. anon, public-edit = same, unlisted = link holders, private = ACL only)
4. Anonymous commenting: allowed by default? Use same fingerprint trailer as anonymous edits (see wikihub-euw3.6)
5. Markdown rendering in comments: full markdown / limited / plain + @mentions
6. Threading depth: flat vs 1-deep replies vs arbitrary
7. Relation to suggested-edits (wikihub-ibt.4): is a suggested edit a kind of comment, or a sibling feature? Recommended: sibling, but both render in the same right-side panel

## Non-goals (v1)
- Reactions / emoji
- Real-time updates (poll or reload is fine)
- Email-reply-to-thread
- Moderation queue UI (just delete/hide actions)

## Output
- docs/comments-design.md committed
- Decisions reflected in follow-up ticket descriptions (data model, UI, etc.)

title: Comment data model + API (CRUD endpoints)
description:
Implement the backend from the design decisions (see sibling design ticket).

## Likely shape (pending design)

Comment model:
- id, wiki_id, page_path, parent_comment_id (nullable for threading if chosen)
- author_id (nullable for anon), author_fingerprint (for anon, same hashing as euw3.6)
- body_markdown, rendered_html_cache
- created_at, updated_at, deleted_at (soft delete)
- block_anchor (nullable, for inline comments if v1 includes them)

## API
- POST   /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/<page>/comments
- GET    /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/<page>/comments
- DELETE /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/<page>/comments/<id>
- PATCH  /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/<page>/comments/<id>  (author edit or owner hide)

Apply the same write rate limits (wikihub-ub2.1), panic-button gate (wikihub-ub2.5), and commit fingerprint pattern (wikihub-euw3.6) used for edits.

## Dependencies
- Design ticket must land first
- Anonymous fingerprint work (wikihub-euw3.6) if anon comments are allowed

title: Comment UI: thread panel on reader view
description:
Render the comment thread on reader pages. Slide-out panel on the right (same visual convention as the Curator panel in wikihub-2jn.1) or inline below page content, per design decision.

## Scope
- Read: list existing comments for the current page, newest-last
- Compose: textarea + Submit, same markdown support as pages (limited per design)
- Anonymous composer: shows 'Posting as anonymous' indicator
- Delete own comment (author) / hide any (owner)
- Empty state: 'No comments yet. Start the conversation.'

## Integration with unified toolbar
Rides on wikihub-zy0h — the 'Comment' toolbar button toggles the panel open/closed; toolbar_state.can_comment gates visibility.

## Files (likely)
- new: app/templates/partials/comments_panel.html
- reader.html: include + toolbar button
- app/static/js/comments.js for compose/submit (minimal, no framework)

title: Anonymous commenting with moderation parity to anonymous edits
description:
Anonymous commenting must use the same moderation primitives as anonymous editing:
- Rate limit per IP (wikihub-ub2.1)
- Panic button gate per wiki (wikihub-ub2.5) — respect Wiki.anonymous_writes_disabled (or a new anonymous_comments_disabled column if decoupling is desired)
- Session fingerprint in DB for revert/hide-by-session actions (wikihub-euw3.6)

## Scope
- Respect the above gates in POST /comments
- Admin action: 'Hide all anonymous comments from this session' (parallel to ub2.6 revert-by-session)
- No CAPTCHA in v1 (rate limit + panic button considered sufficient; revisit if abused)

## Dependencies
- wikihub-euw3.6 (anon fingerprint)
- wikihub-ub2.1 (IP rate limit)
- wikihub-ub2.5 (panic button)

title: Comment notifications: email/in-app when your wiki gets a comment
description:
When a comment is posted on a wiki or page, notify the owner (and @mentioned users if mentions land in v1). Minimum viable: a Notification row + a bell indicator in the header. Email can follow.

## Scope (v1)
- Notification model: id, recipient_user_id, type, payload_json, read_at, created_at
- Trigger: comment-created hook writes one notification per mentioned user + one to the wiki owner (if commenter is not owner)
- Header bell with unread count and simple dropdown list

## Non-goals (v1)
- Email/push notifications — follow-up ticket
- Per-user notification preferences UI — follow-up
- Digest / throttling — follow-up

title: Unify Suggest-Edit with Comments in a shared page-interactions panel
description:
Suggested-edits (wikihub-ibt.4 inline diff UI) and comments are different data types but share a home: a right-side page-interactions panel. Decide whether to render them as sibling tabs in one panel (Comments | Suggestions | History) or a unified 'Activity' stream sorted by time.

## Proposal
Tabbed panel:
- Tab 1: Comments (from wikihub-ub2.2 work)
- Tab 2: Suggestions (from wikihub-ibt.4 work) — pending or recently resolved
- Tab 3: History (short list linking to full /history page — covers wikihub-euw3.5 from the panel side)

Shared chrome, consistent toggle from the toolbar (wikihub-zy0h). Implementing this after Comments lands avoids churn.

## Relation
- wikihub-zy0h (toolbar)
- wikihub-ibt.4 (inline diff / suggested edits)
- wikihub-euw3.5 (history button)

title: Omni-convert upload (PDF/DOCX → markdown)
description: Pandoc handles DOCX→MD well. PDF is messier (pdftotext or LLM). Could ship DOCX-only first as quick win — zip upload infra already exists.

title: Landing page live background
description: Featured/popular wiki cards slowly drifting/orbiting behind hero section. Pure CSS animation. Needs enough public wikis to look good — ship after dogfood migrations populate platform.

title: Panic button: disable anonymous writes per wiki
description: Anti-abuse priority #2. Toggle to instantly disable anonymous writes on a wiki. Quick emergency response for spam/abuse.

title: Revert last N anonymous edits button
description: Anti-abuse priority #3. Web UI button to bulk-revert recent anonymous edits. Recovery path after spam attacks.

title: rate limiting for API write endpoints
description: in-memory sliding window (10 writes/min/user) on all page CRUD endpoints. prevents agent floods from monopolizing gunicorn workers via regenerate_public_mirror.
close_reason: deployed: in-memory sliding window rate limiter, 10 writes/min/user, on create/replace/patch/delete/visibility/bulk endpoints. verified on production — 429s fire correctly after threshold.

title: Fix: shared editors can't see Edit button + visibility/sharing disconnect
close_reason: Edit button now shows for users with @user:edit grants; editor shows collaborator label for non-owners and sharing summary for owners

title: Git remote add UI for owners + push access model for collaborators
description:
## Part 1: Owner "Add Remote" UI (do now)

Add a second command in the clone dropdown, visible only to the wiki owner:

```
CLONE
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──┐
│ git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@u/wiki.git  │⎘│
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──┘

PUSH AN EXISTING REPO (owner only)
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──┐
│ git remote add wikihub https://.../@u/wiki.git     │⎘│
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──┘
```

Plus a subtle info note: "Use your API key as the password when pushing."

Also add documentation on the WikiHub docs wiki (Getting Started or Agent Integration page) explaining git push/pull workflow.

## Part 2: Pathway to GitHub-style collaboration (think about later)

Currently only the wiki owner can push. To get to GitHub-level collaboration:

### What GitHub has that we don't (yet)
1. **Collaborator invites** — owner grants write access to specific users
2. **Fork + PR workflow** — fork a wiki, make changes, submit a pull request
3. **Branch-based PRs** — work on a branch, review diff, merge
4. **Per-branch permissions** — protect main, allow feature branches
5. **Write access via deploy keys** — CI/CD agents can push

### Possible WikiHub pathway
- **Phase 1 (now):** Owner-only push. Clone is public read-only. Fork creates your own copy.
- **Phase 2:** Collaborator model — owner can grant write access to other WikiHub users (stored in .wikihub/acl or a new collaborators table). Collaborators can push to the authoritative repo.
- **Phase 3:** PR-like workflow — forked wikis can propose changes back to the upstream wiki. Owner reviews a diff and merges. Could be lightweight (just a "propose merge" button that shows the diff) rather than full GitHub PRs.
- **Phase 4:** Branch support — multiple branches per wiki, branch-level permissions.

### Key architectural questions
- ACL file already supports per-user permissions — could extend to grant push access
- post-receive hook already syncs git→DB — collaborator pushes would just work
- The two-repo model (auth + public mirror) is already set up for this
- Do we need branches? Or is the fork-and-merge model enough for wikis?

### What's unique to WikiHub
Unlike GitHub, wiki collaboration is often about *content* not *code*. A simpler model might work:
- "Suggest edit" button that creates a pending change (no git knowledge needed)
- Owner approves/rejects inline, like Wikipedia's pending changes
- This would be more accessible than fork+PR for non-technical wiki contributors

## Part 3: Public/Private fork concerns (IMPORTANT)

The two-repo architecture creates real problems for the fork+PR model:

### The core issue
When you fork someone's wiki, you get a copy of the **public mirror** — the derived repo with private pages stripped out. This means:

1. **Information loss on fork**: Private pages, private bands, and .wikihub/acl are absent from the fork. The fork is an incomplete snapshot.

2. **Merge-back conflicts**: If a forked wiki proposes changes back to the upstream, the upstream has files the fork never had. Merging could be messy — the fork's commit history doesn't share a common ancestor with the authoritative repo's full tree.

3. **Accidental exposure risk**: If someone forks a wiki that later makes pages private, the fork still has the old public version of those pages. Forks are snapshots — they don't retroactively remove content. (Same as GitHub, but worth calling out since WikiHub has page-level visibility.)

4. **Collaborator push vs fork-merge**: If a collaborator pushes directly to the authoritative repo, they could theoretically see private pages in the git history. The git backend would need to either:
   - Route collaborators to the authoritative repo (they see everything — simple but scary)
   - Route collaborators to a branch on the authoritative repo (more isolated)
   - Keep collaborators on the public mirror with a merge-request queue (safest, most complex)

5. **ACL sync on merge**: If upstream merges changes from a fork, the ACL file in the fork won't match upstream's ACL. The merge needs to preserve upstream's ACL, not the fork's.

### Recommendations to think about
- Fork should record `forked_from` metadata so we can trace lineage
- "Propose changes" should be a diff-based review, not a git merge — avoids the two-repo mismatch
- Collaborator access should probably be "can push to authoritative repo" (simple) with a trust model (you only invite people you trust with your private pages)
- Consider: should forks of wikis with private pages show a warning? "This fork contains only public pages from the original"

title: External wiki references/aliases in sidebar — cross-wiki navigation
description:
Allow wikis to reference or alias other wikis (even from different users) in their sidebar navigation. Use case: the @wikihub community wiki could have a 'Featured Wikis' folder in its sidebar that lists @jacobcole/rsi-wiki, @jacobcole/admitsphere, etc. as if they were local pages.

This is conceptually like symlinks or bookmarks in a filesystem — you don't own the content, but you can navigate to it from your wiki's sidebar.

Possible implementations:
- A .wikihub/references file that lists external wiki paths to include in sidebar
- A special frontmatter field like 'sidebar_refs' on index.md
- A folder-like virtual entry that links to another wiki

This would make the community index wiki much more useful as a navigational hub — not just a page with links, but actual sidebar entries that feel like part of the wiki.

Inspired by how Quartz and other static site generators handle cross-references.

title: Documented feedback API: POST /api/v1/feedback for users and agents
description:
Wikihub needs a clearly documented endpoint for submitting feedback (bug reports, feature requests, general comments) from both human users and agents. Today there is no canonical path; wikihub-dng (Noos Universal Feedback Widget) covers the embedded UI angle, and wikihub-5b4 covers issue-posting with GitHub/beads sync, but neither defines a simple 'send feedback' surface that an agent can hit reliably.

## Endpoint

POST /api/v1/feedback

Body (application/json):
```json
{
  "kind": "bug" | "feature" | "comment" | "praise",
  "subject": "string (<= 200 chars)",
  "body": "string (<= 10_000 chars, markdown)",
  "context": {
    "page_url": "string (optional)",
    "user_agent": "string (optional)",
    "wiki": "@owner/slug (optional)",
    "extra": { ... }
  },
  "contact_email": "string (optional, for follow-up)"
}
```

Response 201:
```json
{
  "id": "fb_<short-id>",
  "received_at": "2026-04-23T...Z",
  "status": "received"
}
```

## Auth & rate limits
- Public endpoint (no auth required) so anonymous users and unauth'd agents can use it
- Rate limit: 10/min/IP (reuse wikihub-ub2.1 limiter when it lands; per-IP local limiter for now)
- Authenticated requests get a higher limit (60/min) and the feedback is associated with the user

## Storage
- New Feedback model: id, kind, subject, body, context_json, contact_email, user_id (nullable), ip_hash, created_at, status, resolved_at, resolution_notes
- Visible only to admins in v1
- Optional bridge to beads / GitHub Issues (covered by wikihub-5b4) — out of scope here

## Documentation (the 'clearly documented' part)
- docs/api/feedback.md with curl examples, schema, and what happens after submission
- README.md gets a 'Feedback' section linking to /api/v1/feedback and the docs page
- Listed in /api discovery doc (see sibling 'Add /api root endpoint' ticket) under 'feedback'
- Listed in /api/v1/openapi.json once wikihub-o6d lands

## Why P1
Trivial implementation. Agents currently have no idiomatic way to leave feedback after running into issues. This pairs with the /api root discovery doc to make wikihub feel like a real first-class API target for agentic clients.

## Files
- new app/routes/feedback.py
- new app/models/feedback.py
- new docs/api/feedback.md
- README.md update
- registered in app/__init__.py

## Relation
- wikihub-dng (Noos widget) — should ideally call this endpoint server-side
- wikihub-5b4 (issue-posting API + GitHub/beads sync) — feedback can flow into issues with admin approval
- wikihub-o6d (OpenAPI) — feedback endpoint will be in the spec
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP server endpoint (/mcp)
description: Discovery endpoints exist (.well-known/mcp/server-card.json, /.well-known/mcp) but the actual /mcp endpoint serving MCP protocol is missing. Tools: whoami, search, read_page, list_pages, create_page, update_page, append_section, delete_page, set_visibility, share, create_wiki, fork_wiki, commit_log. This is the whole thesis — wikihub is GitHub for LLM wikis.
close_reason: Implemented /mcp with tool listing/call proxy behavior and wired discovery manifests.

title: End-to-end encryption: user-provided keys for wiki content
description:
## Vision

Users can optionally encrypt wiki content with their own keys, so that WikiHub servers never see plaintext for sensitive content. Could apply to specific pages, entire wikis, or all content depending on user preference.

## Open Questions

- **Server-side operations**: The Curator agent, search indexing, and rendering all need plaintext. If content is E2E encrypted, these features can't work on encrypted pages unless the user's key is temporarily available during their session.
- **Key management**: Browser-side encryption (Web Crypto API) vs server-side with user-provided keys? Browser-side is true E2E but breaks git clone workflow. Server-side with user keys is simpler but the server sees plaintext during the session.
- **Granularity**: Per-page visibility already exists (public/private/unlisted). E2E encryption could be a 4th tier: "encrypted" — stored encrypted at rest, decrypted only in the user's browser.
- **Search**: Encrypted pages can't be full-text indexed. Accept this limitation? Or use client-side search?
- **Collaboration**: If a page is E2E encrypted, how do collaborators decrypt? Shared keys? Per-user encryption with key exchange?
- **Git compatibility**: Encrypted content in git repos would be binary blobs, breaking diff/merge. Alternative: git-crypt or similar?

## Possible Approaches

1. **Browser-only decryption** (true E2E): Web Crypto API encrypts/decrypts in browser. Server stores ciphertext. No search, no Curator, no rendering server-side for encrypted pages.
2. **Session-key model**: User provides key on login, server holds it in memory for the session, encrypts at rest. Server sees plaintext during session but not at rest.
3. **Hybrid**: Public pages are plaintext (searchable, Curator-accessible). Private pages can optionally be E2E encrypted with browser-only decryption.

## v2 — not blocking any current work

title: Zip upload creates wiki but UI stays on create page
description: When uploading a zip via /new, the wiki is actually created successfully, but the UI doesn't redirect — it spins briefly then stays on the create page as if something went wrong. Visual-only bug; the wiki exists if you navigate to it manually.
close_reason: Fixed: use redirect:manual in fetch for drag-drop zip upload so browser navigates to the new wiki.

title: Incremental public mirror update instead of full rebuild on every page write
description: Every page create/update/delete calls regenerate_public_mirror which rebuilds the entire mirror from scratch (ls-tree all files, cat-file each, hash-object each, update-index each). For a wiki with N pages, that's ~3N git subprocess calls. Add an incremental update_mirror_page() that loads the existing mirror tree with read-tree, then adds/updates/removes just the one changed file. Full rebuild stays for ACL changes, bulk ops, renames.

title: [Agent UX] Raise write rate limit for authenticated agents + log 429s + review past violations
description:
# Context

Rate limiter lives in `app/auth_utils.py` line 103:
```python
def rate_limit_writes(max_per_minute=10):
```
Applied on `POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/pages` via `@rate_limit_writes()` (see `app/routes/api_wikis.py:370`).

Agents doing legitimate bulk imports (e.g. pushing an existing markdown tree to a new wiki) hit 10/min trivially. Example from 2026-04-23: Claude agent importing picortex's 31 planning docs to `jacobcole/picortex` hit 429s repeatedly and had to pace with 7s sleeps.

# Three sub-tasks

## 1. Raise / tier the rate limit for authenticated agents
Options:
- Flat raise (e.g. 30/min or 60/min for any API-key-authed writer)
- Tiered by scope: bulk-import scope (60/min) vs interactive (10/min)
- Per-wiki-creation burst allowance (first N writes after POST /wikis are cheap)
- `X-Agent-Bulk: true` request header that opts into stricter quotas per 5-min window instead of per-minute

Recommendation: tiered with a documented "bulk-import" mode, because legitimate 1-per-minute interactive use should stay capped.

## 2. Log rate-limit violations
Currently the 429 is returned silently — no `logger.warning`, no DB row, no metric. Add structured logging:
- Fields: `user_id`, `username`, `ip`, `route`, `method`, `wiki_slug` (if parseable), `timestamp`, `user_agent`
- Destination: structured log line tagged `event_type=rate_limit.violation` + optional `RateLimitEvent` table for easy querying
- Don't log request bodies (could contain secrets)

## 3. Review past violations
**Blocked on sub-task 2** — there's currently no log to review. Once logging lands, after 1-2 weeks of data, produce a short report:
- 429 count by user / endpoint / hour-of-day
- Which 429s were legitimate bulk imports (should be allowed) vs abuse (should stay blocked)
- Proposed new limits calibrated on observed data

# Done when

- [ ] Rate-limit logs appear for every 429
- [ ] Bulk-import mode (or raise) shipped so Claude-agent wiki imports run without artificial pacing
- [ ] Report reviewed and new limits tuned

# Related

- `app/auth_utils.py` lines 103-132 (rate limiter)
- `app/__init__.py` line 175 (global 429 handler, also silent)
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py:370` (decorator applied to page creation)

title: Page-actions toolbar: unify Edit/History/Star/Fork/Comment/Request-Access into one context-driven component
description:
Today the page-actions bar in reader.html (lines 265-322) has three disconnected conditional islands — owner block (Edit, New folder), authenticated non-owner block (Star, Fork), and nothing for anonymous visitors — plus missing buttons (History, Request Access, Comment). The template ignores user_can_edit even though wiki.py:916 computes it.

## Proposal

One computed toolbar_state dict passed from the route:

```
toolbar_state = {
  can_edit: bool,              # user_can_edit
  can_manage: bool,            # is_owner (New folder, visibility, remote)
  can_star: bool,              # authenticated and not owner
  can_comment: bool,           # per ub2.2 ACL
  show_history: bool,          # page has commits
  show_request_access: bool,   # not can_edit and vis in ('public','private')
  show_signin_cta: bool,       # anon and can_edit and owner-wants-auth'd-edits
}
```

Template becomes a flat list of `{% if toolbar_state.X %}` blocks — one place to reason about visibility, no user-type layering. Same state feeds the folder view and wiki index.

## Why it matters

- Eliminates the current gap where user_can_edit is computed but ignored
- Single place to evolve as comments, suggest-edits, request-access land
- Consistent behavior across reader / folder / wiki index views
- Easier to test

## Relation
- Superset container for public-edit workflow fixes (see umbrella ticket)
- Landing pad for comments UI (wikihub-ub2.2) and request-access CTA
- Should land after or alongside the bvl fix to avoid churn
